docs.title: Quick Reference — Commands You Run Every Session
docs.text:
# Before writing any code
.venv\Scripts\activate

# After writing code
ruff check agentstatelib/        # catch style issues immediately
mypy agentstatelib/              # catch type errors immediately
pytest tests/ -v              # run all tests

# Before committing
ruff check agentstatelib/
mypy agentstatelib/ --strict
pytest tests/ -v --tb=short
git add .
git commit -m "type: description"

# Commit message prefixes:
# feat: new feature
# fix: bug fix
# refactor: code change with no behavior change
# test: adding tests
# docs: documentation
# chore: tooling, config, dependencies

docs.title: Package naming — read this first, refer back always
docs.text:
Two names. Never mix them up.

Install from PyPI:

pip install agentstate-lib

Import in Python:

from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.core.state import SharedState
from agentstatelib.coordination.conflicts import LastWriteWins

Source folder on disk: agentstatelib/

In pyproject.toml the project name is agentstate-lib (hyphens). The importable package is agentstatelib (underscores). Every import in every file in this document uses agentstatelib. Every pip install uses agentstate-lib.

docs.title: How to read this document
docs.text:
Every week follows the same structure:

  • Context — why this week matters and what it enables
  • Files to work in — exact paths in the order you write them
  • Detailed instructions per file — imports, classes, methods, fields, docstrings — everything except the actual code characters
  • Docs to write — what user-facing documentation ships with this version
  • Test commands — exact PowerShell commands to run
  • Publish commands — how to ship and deploy docs
  • Done when — explicit pass criteria before moving to the next week

Rules: 1. Run ruff check, mypy --strict, and pytest after every coding session — not once per week, every session 2. Every public class and function gets a docstring — mkdocstrings renders these into your API reference pages 3. Docs ship with every published version — users who install the day you publish must be able to use it immediately 4. Colab is your personal GPU dev tool — it ships nothing to users

docs.title: Competitive Positioning
docs.text:
You build against LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen. Every design decision reinforces one of three things:

Coordination — typed SharedState, StatePatch pipeline, batch conflict resolution, invariant checking. LangGraph silently merges state with dict reducers. You detect and log every conflict explicitly.

Observability — append-only event log, OpenTelemetry traces, replay debugger, live dashboard. No existing multi-agent framework ships this as a first-class built-in feature.

Framework agnosticism — any async callable returning StatePatch is a valid agent. OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, Groq, rule-based functions — all identical from your library's perspective. LangGraph is coupled to LangChain.

Feature                   | agentstatelib | LangGraph | CrewAI | AutoGen
--------------------------|---------------|-----------|--------|--------
Typed shared state        | yes           | dict only | no     | no
Append-only event log     | yes           | no        | no     | no
Conflict detection        | yes           | no        | no     | no
Parallel round execution  | yes           | partial   | no     | no
Replay debugger           | yes           | no        | no     | no
Framework agnostic        | yes           | no        | part   | part
Local model support       | yes           | partial   | part   | part
Built-in observability    | yes           | no        | no     | no

docs.title: Documentation Strategy
docs.text:
MkDocs + Material theme + mkdocstrings + mike. This is the same stack FastAPI and Pydantic use.

  • MkDocs generates a static site from Markdown files hosted free on GitHub Pages
  • mkdocstrings reads Python docstrings and generates API reference pages automatically — every docstring you write in source becomes a searchable reference page, which is why docstrings matter from day one
  • mike manages versioned docs so users can read docs for the exact version installed

Doc site grows each week:

docs/
├── index.md                      ← comparison table + install
├── quickstart.md                 ← 15-line working example
├── concepts/
│   ├── shared-state.md           ← week 1
│   ├── patches.md                ← week 2
│   ├── conflict.md               ← week 3
│   ├── parallelism.md            ← week 3
│   ├── event-log.md              ← week 5
│   └── observability.md          ← week 7
├── api-reference/
│   ├── state.md                  ← week 1
│   ├── patch.md                  ← week 2
│   ├── graph.md                  ← week 2
│   ├── conflicts.md              ← week 3
│   ├── store.md                  ← week 5
│   └── observability.md          ← week 7
├── guides/
│   ├── llm-integration.md        ← week 6
│   ├── local-models.md           ← week 6
│   ├── checkpoint-recovery.md    ← week 5
│   ├── opentelemetry.md          ← week 7
│   ├── dashboard.md              ← week 8
│   ├── testing.md                ← week 9
│   ├── production.md             ← week 10
│   ├── integrations.md           ← week 12
│   └── migration/v0.x-to-v1.md  ← week 12
├── decisions/                    ← ADRs week 11
├── benchmark.md                  ← week 6
└── design.md                     ← week 13

docs.title: Local Models: Your Dev Setup vs What Ships to Users
docs.text:
Your Colab session is your personal GPU access for testing. It ships nothing to users.

What you use (dev only): - notebooks/colab_setup.ipynb — starts Ollama on Colab Pro, creates ngrok tunnel to your Windows machine - .env with COLAB_OLLAMA_URL=https://xxxx.ngrok-free.app - The ngrok URL changes every Colab session — update .env each time

What ships to users: - agentstatelib/contrib/base_agent.py — retry-with-correction base class, provider-agnostic - examples/models/ — reference implementations users copy and adapt - docs/guides/llm-integration.md — integration guide written for users - docs/guides/local-models.md — Ollama grammar-constrained output guide

Users bring their own Ollama on their own server, their own API keys, their own inference setup. Your library only sees StatePatch objects — it never cares where they came from.

Colab session workflow: 1. Open notebooks/colab_setup.ipynb in Colab Pro 2. Runtime → Run all (or run cells 1–6 in order) 3. Copy the ngrok URL printed by Cell 4 4. Update .env: COLAB_OLLAMA_URL=https://xxxx.ngrok-free.app 5. Keep Cell 6 running to prevent timeout

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Set up so every tool runs clean on an empty codebase. If anything fails here, fix it before writing library code — undetected config problems become confusing failures weeks later.

docs.title: Files to create
docs.text:
pyproject.toml — replace the generated file entirely.

[build-system] section: requires = ["hatchling"], build-backend = "hatchling.build".

[project] section: name = "agentstate-lib", version = "0.1.0", description = "Typed stateful coordination layer for parallel multi-agent AI systems", readme = "README.md", requires-python = ">=3.11", dependencies = ["pydantic>=2.0", "aiosqlite>=0.19"]. Add authors with your name and email. Add license = {text = "MIT"}.

[project.optional-dependencies] section with these groups: - api contains fastapi>=0.100 and uvicorn with standard extras - otel contains opentelemetry-api, opentelemetry-sdk, opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-grpc - dashboard contains rich>=13.0 - contrib is an empty list — the contrib module has no extra deps - docs contains mkdocs-material>=9.0, mkdocstrings with python extra, mike>=2.0 - dev contains pytest>=7.0, pytest-asyncio>=0.21, mypy>=1.0, ruff>=0.1, hypothesis>=6.0, httpx>=0.24, python-dotenv>=1.0

[tool.ruff] section: line-length = 88, target-version = "py312". Under [tool.ruff.lint]: select = ["E", "F", "I", "UP", "B"].

[tool.mypy] section: python_version = "3.12", strict = true, ignore_missing_imports = true.

[tool.pytest.ini_options] section: asyncio_mode = "auto", testpaths = ["tests"].

All package __init__.py files — create empty files at each of these paths: agentstatelib/__init__.py, agentstatelib/core/__init__.py, agentstatelib/router/__init__.py, agentstatelib/coordination/__init__.py, agentstatelib/memory/__init__.py, agentstatelib/observability/__init__.py, agentstatelib/api/__init__.py, agentstatelib/contrib/__init__.py, tests/__init__.py, tests/unit/__init__.py, tests/integration/__init__.py, tests/property/__init__.py.

Write a single comment in agentstatelib/__init__.py: # Public API — exports added as modules are built. Write a single comment in tests/conftest.py: # Shared fixtures — populated starting week 1.

.gitignore — add these entries: .venv/, __pycache__/, *.pyc, .mypy_cache/, .ruff_cache/, .pytest_cache/, dist/, *.egg-info/, .env, *.db, .checkpoints/, site/.

.vscode/settings.json — set python.defaultInterpreterPath to .venv/Scripts/python.exe, editor.formatOnSave to true, editor.defaultFormatter to charliermarsh.ruff, python.testing.pytestEnabled to true, python.testing.pytestArgs to ["tests"].

.env — create on Windows, add to .gitignore, never commit. Four lines:

OPENAI_API_KEY=
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
GROQ_API_KEY=
COLAB_OLLAMA_URL=http://localhost:11434

mkdocs.yml — create in project root. Set site_name to agentstatelib. Set site_description. Set repo_url to your GitHub URL. Under theme: name is material, features list includes navigation.tabs, navigation.sections, content.code.copy, search.suggest. Under plugins: list search and mkdocstrings with handlers → python → options → show_source: true. Under extra: version → provider: mike. Nav for now: Home pointing to index.md and Quickstart pointing to quickstart.md.

docs/index.md — write the landing page. Two sentences describing the library. The full comparison table above formatted as a Markdown table. Install command pip install agentstate-lib. Import line from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch. Link to quickstart.

docs/quickstart.md — one line: Coming with v0.1.0.

Create placeholder files in docs/concepts/ for all concept pages listed in the doc structure. Each contains one line: Coming in version X.Y.

notebooks/colab_setup.ipynb — create this notebook with six cells.

Cell 1 installs Ollama and the library. Contains two shell commands: the curl command for the Ollama install script, and pip install of pyngrok and agentstate-lib.

Cell 2 starts the Ollama server. Creates a subprocess.Popen call with ["ollama", "serve"], stdout and stderr both redirected to DEVNULL. Sleeps 3 seconds. Makes a GET request to http://localhost:11434/api/tags with timeout=5. Prints the list of model names from the response. Prints an error message if the request fails.

Cell 3 detects GPU type using nvidia-smi with the query flags for name and memory.total and format csv,noheader. Builds a models list starting with llama3:8b and mistral:7b, adding llama3:70b only if "A100" appears in the GPU output. For each model in the list: runs ollama pull {model}, prints "ready" if returncode is 0, prints "FAILED" with the stderr if not.

Cell 4 kills any existing ngrok tunnels with ngrok.kill(). Creates a new HTTP tunnel to port 11434. Prints the public URL in a clearly delimited block. Prints the exact line to add to the Windows .env file. Prints a note that the URL changes every session.

Cell 5 is a smoke test. POSTs to http://localhost:11434/api/generate with model llama3:8b, a prompt asking for a specific JSON object, and stream=False. Prints the response field from the JSON. Prints "Ollama working."

Cell 6 is a keep-alive. Prints "Keep-alive running. Do not stop this cell." then loops forever: sleep 60 seconds, increment counter, every 5 iterations print the elapsed minutes.

docs.title: Phase 0 commands
docs.text:
uv add pydantic aiosqlite python-dotenv
uv add --optional docs mkdocs-material "mkdocstrings[python]" mike
uv add --dev pytest pytest-asyncio mypy ruff hypothesis httpx

ruff check agentstatelib/
mypy agentstatelib/
pytest tests/ -v
python -c "import agentstatelib; print('import ok')"

mkdocs build --strict

git add .
git commit -m "chore: initial scaffold"
git push -u origin main

docs.title: Done when
docs.text: python -c "import agentstatelib" prints import ok. mkdocs build --strict succeeds and produces site/. All four tool commands exit with code 0. The Colab notebook cells 1–5 run without errors when opened in Colab Pro.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
This week you build the data foundation. SharedState is the shared world model. StateEvent is the typed record of everything that happens to it. SQLiteStore is how events persist across process restarts. Get the shapes right here — changing serialized event schemas later is painful.

Docstring discipline starts now. mkdocstrings pulls docstrings into API reference pages. Every class and every public function needs a docstring. A class without one produces a blank API page.

docs.title: Files to work in
docs.text:
1. agentstatelib/core/state.py
  2. agentstatelib/core/events.py
  3. agentstatelib/memory/store.py
  4. agentstatelib/memory/__init__.py
  5. agentstatelib/core/__init__.py
  6. tests/unit/test_state.py
  7. tests/unit/test_events.py
  8. tests/unit/test_store.py
  9. docs/concepts/shared-state.md
  10. docs/api-reference/state.md
  11. docs/api-reference/events.md

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/state.py
docs.text:
Import BaseModel and Field from pydantic. Import Literal and Any from typing. Import uuid and time.

Write WorkflowStatus as a type alias using Literal with exactly four string values: "running", "complete", "failed", "paused". This is the complete set of valid workflow statuses — no other values are accepted.

Write the Task model inheriting from BaseModel. Fields in order: - id typed as str with default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()) - goal_id typed as str | None defaulting to None — tasks with no goal_id are ungrouped and not subject to goal-based invariant checks - description typed as str - status typed as Literal["pending", "running", "done", "failed"] defaulting to "pending" - result typed as Any | None defaulting to None - created_at typed as float with default_factory=time.time - updated_at typed as float with default_factory=time.time

Docstring for Task: "A unit of work within a workflow. May optionally belong to a Goal via goal_id. When goal_id is None the task is ungrouped and not checked by goal-based invariants. The result field stores agent output after the task completes."

Write the Goal model. Fields: - id typed as str, uuid factory - description typed as str - status typed as Literal["pending", "active", "complete", "failed"] defaulting to "pending" - created_at typed as float, time factory

Docstring for Goal: "A high-level objective that groups related Tasks. Goals are optional — workflows that use only facts and artifacts do not need them."

Write the Artifact model. Fields: - id typed as str, uuid factory - produced_by typed as str — the agent_id that created this artifact - artifact_type typed as str — free-form, examples: draft, source, summary, decision, report - content typed as Any - created_at typed as float, time factory

Docstring for Artifact: "An output produced by an agent and stored in shared state. artifact_type is a free-form string — use whatever names make sense for your workflow domain, such as 'draft', 'source', or 'summary'."

Write the Decision model. Fields: - id typed as str, uuid factory - made_by typed as str - description typed as str - rationale typed as str — the reasoning behind the decision, required for audit - timestamp typed as float, time factory

Docstring for Decision: "A recorded workflow decision with rationale. Append to SharedState.decisions when an agent makes a significant choice that downstream agents or human reviewers should be able to audit."

Write the SharedState model. Fields in order: - workflow_id typed as str, uuid factory - workflow_type typed as str defaulting to "general" - goal typed as str - goals typed as dict[str, Goal] with default_factory=dict - tasks typed as dict[str, Task] with default_factory=dict - artifacts typed as dict[str, Artifact] with default_factory=dict - decisions typed as list[Decision] with default_factory=list - facts typed as dict[str, Any] with default_factory=dict - status typed as WorkflowStatus defaulting to "running" - created_at typed as float, time factory - updated_at typed as float, time factory

Docstring for SharedState: "The single source of truth for a workflow. Never mutated directly — all changes go through StatePatch and apply_patch which return a new SharedState object. All agents in a parallel round read the same SharedState snapshot, guaranteeing consistent reads within a round with no intra-round races. The event log stores the sequence of patches applied to state, so any past state can be reconstructed by replaying the log."

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/events.py
docs.text:
Import BaseModel, Field, TypeAdapter from pydantic. Import Literal, Annotated, Union, Any from typing. Import uuid and time.

Write BaseStateEvent inheriting from BaseModel. Fields: - event_id typed as str, uuid factory - workflow_id typed as str - agent_id typed as str - timestamp typed as float, time factory

Docstring for BaseStateEvent: "Base class for all events in the agentstatelib event log. Every state change, conflict, checkpoint, and error produces a typed event appended to the store. Events are immutable once written."

Write six event subclasses, each inheriting from BaseStateEvent. For each one, add a type field typed as a Literal with a single string value and that same string as the default. This discriminator field is what allows Pydantic to deserialize any serialized event back to the correct subclass.

WorkflowStarted — type: Literal["workflow_started"] = "workflow_started". Extra fields: workflow_type typed as str, goal typed as str. Docstring: "Emitted at the start of AgentGraph.run(). Marks the beginning of the event sequence and carries the workflow metadata needed to reconstruct SharedState from scratch during replay."

WorkflowCompleted — type: Literal["workflow_completed"] = "workflow_completed". Extra field: final_status typed as str. Docstring: "Emitted when AgentGraph.run() finishes normally. final_status is the workflow status at the time of completion."

PatchApplied — type: Literal["patch_applied"] = "patch_applied". Extra fields: patch_id typed as str, target typed as str, old_value typed as Any, new_value typed as Any, reason typed as str. Note that timestamp is inherited from BaseStateEvent but here it carries the patch's own timestamp — the PatchApplied event's timestamp is the event creation time, not the patch creation time, so you may need to store the patch timestamp separately if needed. Docstring: "Emitted after a winning patch is applied to state. old_value and new_value store only the value at patch.target — not full state dumps. This keeps individual events compact regardless of overall state size."

ConflictDetected — type: Literal["conflict_detected"] = "conflict_detected". Extra fields: conflict_id typed as str, path typed as str, winner_agent_id typed as str, loser_agent_id typed as str, resolution_strategy typed as str, existing_patch typed as dict[str, object] | None defaulting to None, incoming_patch typed as dict[str, object] | None defaulting to None. Docstring: "Emitted when resolve_batch() detects two patches targeting the same state path in the same parallel round. Both patches stored as plain dicts rather than StatePatch objects to guarantee JSON serializability regardless of what value fields contain."

CheckpointSaved — type: Literal["checkpoint_saved"] = "checkpoint_saved". Extra fields: checkpoint_id typed as str, event_count typed as int. Docstring: "Emitted when save_checkpoint() completes successfully."

AgentErrored — type: Literal["agent_errored"] = "agent_errored". Extra fields: error_type typed as str, error_message typed as str, retry_count typed as int. Docstring: "Emitted when an agent raises an unhandled exception. retry_count records how many retries were attempted."

After the six subclasses, write StateEvent as a type alias. Use Annotated wrapping a Union of all six subclass types, with Field(discriminator="type") as the second argument to Annotated. This is the discriminated union — Pydantic uses the type field value to pick the correct subclass during deserialization.

Write at module level: event_adapter = TypeAdapter(StateEvent). This is the object used for deserialization — callers call event_adapter.validate_json(raw_json_string) to get back the correct typed event subclass.

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/store.py
docs.text:
Import Protocol and runtime_checkable from typing. Import aiosqlite. Import json. Import StateEvent and event_adapter from agentstatelib.core.events. Import BaseStateEvent too.

Write the StateStore protocol. Decorate it with @runtime_checkable. It should have four async methods: append(self, event: StateEvent) -> None, get_workflow(self, workflow_id: str) -> list[StateEvent], since(self, workflow_id: str, index: int) -> list[StateEvent], count(self, workflow_id: str) -> int. Each method body is just .... Docstring for StateStore: "Protocol for agentstatelib persistence backends. Any class implementing these four async methods satisfies this protocol and works as a drop-in backend for AgentGraph. No base class import required — structural typing means any conforming class qualifies automatically. Built-in implementations: InMemoryStore (for testing), SQLiteStore (for single-process use), PostgreSQLStore (for production)."

Write InMemoryStore. It has one instance variable _events: dict[str, list[StateEvent]] initialized to an empty dict in __init__. Implement all four methods: - append adds the event to the list for that workflow_id, creating the list if the key does not yet exist - get_workflow returns a copy of the list (copy is critical — callers must not mutate the store's internal state), or an empty list if the workflow_id is not found - since returns the list sliced from the given index onward, or an empty list if the workflow_id is not found - count returns the length of the list, or 0 if the workflow_id is not found

Docstring for InMemoryStore: "In-memory StateStore. Data is lost when the process exits. Use for tests and rapid development. Satisfies the StateStore protocol and passes the same test suite as SQLiteStore."

Write SQLiteStore. It takes a path: str parameter in __init__, stored as self.path. Write a private async method _init_db(self, db) that executes CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS events with columns: id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, event_id TEXT NOT NULL, workflow_id TEXT NOT NULL, type TEXT NOT NULL, data TEXT NOT NULL, timestamp REAL NOT NULL. Then executes CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_wf_id ON events(workflow_id). Then calls await db.commit().

Implement append: open a connection with async with aiosqlite.connect(self.path) as db, call await self._init_db(db), execute an INSERT with the five non-id fields, then await db.commit(). Serialize the event to JSON using event.model_dump_json(). The INSERT statement inserts event_id, workflow_id, event.type, the serialized JSON string, and event.timestamp.

Implement get_workflow: open connection with async with aiosqlite.connect(self.path) as db, call await self._init_db(db), execute SELECT data FROM events WHERE workflow_id=? ORDER BY id ASC with the workflow_id as the parameter, call await cursor.fetchall(), return a list where each row's data column (row[0]) is deserialized with event_adapter.validate_json(row[0]).

Implement since: same structure as get_workflow but the query adds LIMIT -1 OFFSET ? with the index as the second parameter. LIMIT -1 in SQLite means no limit on results, and OFFSET ? skips the first index rows. Return the deserialized list.

Implement count: open connection, init db, execute SELECT COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE workflow_id=?, call await cursor.fetchone(), return row[0] as an integer.

Docstring for SQLiteStore: "SQLite-backed StateStore. Events persist across process restarts. Each write is wrapped in a transaction. Suitable for single-process workflows and development. For production multi-process deployments, use PostgreSQLStore (requires asyncpg)."

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/__init__.py
docs.text: Import StateStore, InMemoryStore, SQLiteStore from agentstatelib.memory.store. Set __all__ to a list containing all three names.

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/__init__.py
docs.text:
Import and re-export from agentstatelib.core.state: SharedState, Task, Goal, Artifact, Decision, WorkflowStatus.

Import and re-export from agentstatelib.core.events: StateEvent, BaseStateEvent, WorkflowStarted, WorkflowCompleted, PatchApplied, ConflictDetected, CheckpointSaved, AgentErrored, event_adapter.

Set __all__ listing every name.

docs.title: tests/unit/test_state.py
docs.text:
Import pytest. Import ValidationError from pydantic. Import SharedState, Task, Goal, WorkflowStatus from agentstatelib.core.state.

Write four test functions:

test_shared_state_defaults — create SharedState(goal="test goal"). Assert s.workflow_id is a non-empty string. Assert s.tasks is an empty dict. Assert s.artifacts is an empty dict. Assert s.status == "running".

test_shared_state_rejects_invalid_status — use pytest.raises(ValidationError) as a context manager and inside create SharedState(goal="x", status="invalid_status"). The ValidationError must be raised — if it is not, the test fails.

test_task_defaults — create Task(description="do something"). Assert t.status == "pending". Assert t.result is None. Assert t.goal_id is None. Assert t.id is a non-empty string.

test_two_states_have_different_ids — create two SharedState objects both with goal="same goal". Assert s1.workflow_id != s2.workflow_id.

docs.title: tests/unit/test_events.py
docs.text:
Import pytest. Import ValidationError from pydantic. Import all event types and event_adapter from agentstatelib.core.events.

Write five test functions:

test_patch_applied_round_trip — create a PatchApplied event with all required fields filled in. Call .model_dump_json() to get a JSON string. Call event_adapter.validate_json(json_string). Assert the recovered object is an instance of PatchApplied. Assert recovered.event_id == original.event_id.

test_discriminated_union_selects_correct_type — create a WorkflowStarted event. Serialize it with .model_dump_json(). Deserialize with event_adapter.validate_json(). Assert the result is an instance of WorkflowStarted and is not an instance of PatchApplied or any other subclass.

test_all_event_types_serialize — create one instance of each of the six event types with all required fields. For each: call .model_dump_json() then event_adapter.validate_json() on the result. Assert no exceptions are raised. Assert recovered.event_id == original.event_id for each.

test_base_fields_on_all_events — create one instance of each event type. For each: assert hasattr(event, "event_id"), hasattr(event, "workflow_id"), hasattr(event, "agent_id"), hasattr(event, "timestamp").

test_invalid_type_raises — use pytest.raises(ValidationError) and inside call event_adapter.validate_json('{"type": "not_a_real_type", "workflow_id": "x", "agent_id": "y", "event_id": "z", "timestamp": 1.0}').

docs.title: tests/unit/test_store.py
docs.text:
Import pytest. Import pytest_asyncio mark. Import WorkflowStarted, PatchApplied, event_adapter from agentstatelib.core.events. Import InMemoryStore, SQLiteStore, StateStore from agentstatelib.memory.store.

Write a pytest fixture in_memory_store that returns InMemoryStore().

Write a pytest fixture sqlite_store that takes the built-in tmp_path fixture and returns SQLiteStore(str(tmp_path / "test.db")).

Write five tests, each parametrized with @pytest.mark.parametrize("store_fixture", ["in_memory_store", "sqlite_store"]). Inside each test, get the actual store with store = request.getfixturevalue(store_fixture). Mark all tests with @pytest.mark.asyncio.

test_append_and_retrieve — create a WorkflowStarted event. await store.append(event). events = await store.get_workflow(event.workflow_id). Assert len(events) == 1. Assert events[0].event_id == event.event_id.

test_unknown_workflow_returns_empty — events = await store.get_workflow("workflow-that-does-not-exist"). Assert events == [].

test_count_increments — append three events all with the same workflow_id. count = await store.count(workflow_id). Assert count == 3.

test_since_returns_from_index — append four events to the same workflow_id. result = await store.since(workflow_id, 2). Assert len(result) == 2. The result should contain the 3rd and 4th events (0-indexed offset 2).

test_event_type_preserved_after_retrieve — append a PatchApplied event. Retrieve it. Assert the retrieved event is an instance of PatchApplied — not just a BaseStateEvent.

Write one additional non-parametrized test:

test_both_stores_satisfy_protocol — assert isinstance(InMemoryStore(), StateStore) is True. Assert isinstance(SQLiteStore(":memory:"), StateStore) is True. This verifies @runtime_checkable is working.

docs.title: docs/concepts/shared-state.md
docs.text:
Write the SharedState concept guide for users.

Section 1 — What SharedState is. Introduce it as the shared world model all agents in a workflow read from. Use the blackboard analogy: every agent reads from the same blackboard and proposes updates to it, but nothing is written directly — proposals go through a validation and conflict-resolution process first.

Section 2 — Why it is never mutated directly. Explain that every change goes through StatePatch and apply_patch, which return a new SharedState object. The original never changes. Explain what this enables: every change is attributed and logged, replaying events reconstructs any past state, parallel agents see the same consistent snapshot within a round, conflicts between agents can be detected before any state is modified.

Section 3 — The fields. Walk through goals, tasks, artifacts, facts, and decisions with one realistic example of when to use each. Keep examples grounded — a research workflow using goals to track objectives, tasks for concrete steps, artifacts for produced documents, facts for shared configuration, decisions for choices that need audit trails.

Section 4 — Code example. A literal fenced code block showing: create a SharedState, apply a patch, assert the original is unchanged. Use real import paths. Users should be able to copy and run this.

Section 5 — Next steps. Link to the Patches concept page.

docs.title: docs/api-reference/state.md

docs.title: Phase 1 · Week 2 · StatePatch · AgentGraph · Publish v0.1.0
Context
docs.text: This week you build the two most important user-facing components. StatePatch is the mutation proposal — the contract between agents and the coordination layer. AgentGraph is the execution engine implementing the parallel round model that is your core architectural differentiator. The quickstart docs ship this week. Users who install v0.1.3 must have working code within five minutes of reading the docs.

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/patch.py
docs.text:
Import BaseModel and Field from pydantic. Import Any from typing. Import uuid and time. Import TYPE_CHECKING from typing — use this to import SharedState only for type checking to avoid circular imports: if TYPE_CHECKING: from agentstatelib.core.state import SharedState.

Write the StatePatch model inheriting from BaseModel. Fields in order: - patch_id typed as str with default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()) - agent_id typed as str — the agent that produced this patch - target typed as str — a dotted path like "tasks.plan.status" or "artifacts.draft.content" - value typed as Any — the new value to set at the target path - reason typed as str — one sentence explaining why this update is being made - timestamp typed as float with default_factory=time.time - priority typed as int defaulting to 0 — used by PriorityBased conflict resolver, higher value wins

Docstring for StatePatch: "A structured state update proposal returned by an agent. Agents never write to SharedState directly — they return a StatePatch. The library validates the patch, resolves conflicts with other patches from the same round, applies the winner to state, and logs a PatchApplied event. The reason field is required for a meaningful audit trail."

Write set_nested(obj: dict, path: str, value: Any) -> dict. This function splits path on ".". It walks the dict one part at a time. For each part except the last: if that key does not exist in the current level, set it to an empty dict, then step into it. For the last part, set the value. Return the original obj — the mutation is in-place. Never use eval() or exec(). Docstring with example: set_nested({}, "tasks.t1.status", "done") returns {"tasks": {"t1": {"status": "done"}}}.

Write get_nested(obj: dict, path: str) -> Any. This function splits path on ".". It walks the dict one part at a time. If at any point the current value is not a dict or the key is missing, return None without raising. Return the value at the final key. Docstring with example: get_nested({"tasks": {"t1": {"status": "done"}}}, "tasks.t1.status") returns "done". get_nested({}, "a.b.c") returns None.

Write apply_patch(state: "SharedState", patch: StatePatch) -> "SharedState". Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state inside the function body to avoid circular import at module load time. Call state.model_dump() to get a plain dict. Call set_nested on that dict with patch.target and patch.value. Call SharedState.model_validate(modified_dict) and return the result. The original state object must never be modified — verify this by checking that state.model_dump() produces the same output before and after the call. Docstring: "Apply a StatePatch to a SharedState, returning a new SharedState. The original state object is never modified. This immutability is what makes event replay and conflict detection possible — any previous state can be reconstructed."

docs.title: agentstatelib/router/types.py
docs.text:
Import Callable and Awaitable from typing. Import TYPE_CHECKING from typing. Under if TYPE_CHECKING: import StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch.

Write a module-level docstring: "Type aliases defining the contracts for agent functions and edge conditions in agentstatelib."

Write AgentFn as a type alias: AgentFn = Callable[[dict], Awaitable["StatePatch"]]. Docstring: "The complete interface for an agentstatelib agent. Any async function accepting a context dict and returning a StatePatch satisfies this type. No class inheritance required. No library imports required inside the agent function. This design allows agents backed by OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, rule-based Python, or any other callable to work identically."

Write EdgeCondition as a type alias: EdgeCondition = Callable[[dict], bool]. Docstring: "A function that receives the current state as a plain dict and returns True if the edge should fire. Edge conditions are evaluated after each round completes to determine which agents run next."

docs.title: agentstatelib/router/context.py
docs.text:
Import Any from typing. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import get_nested and set_nested from agentstatelib.core.patch.

Write slice_state(state: SharedState, include_paths: list[str]) -> dict. Convert state to a plain dict with state.model_dump(). Create an empty result dict. For each path in include_paths: call get_nested(full_dict, path) — if the result is not None, call set_nested(result, path, result_value) to place it at the same dotted path in the result dict. Return the result dict. If include_paths is empty, return the full state dict. Docstring: "Extract a subset of SharedState by dotted path. Each agent node declares which paths it needs via the context= parameter of @graph.node(). The agent receives exactly those paths — nothing more. Serves two purposes: limits context window usage for LLM-backed agents, and prevents agents from accidentally reading state they should not see."

docs.title: agentstatelib/router/graph.py
docs.text:
This is the most important file. Use the parallel round-based implementation from the review session — the version with _execute_round, _run_agents_parallel, _next_round, _emit, and resolve_batch. Every public method needs a complete docstring.

Imports at the top: asyncio, Callable from collections.abc, dataclass from dataclasses. Then all imports from your own modules.

Write WorkflowEvent as a type alias: the union of PatchApplied | WorkflowStarted | WorkflowCompleted | ConflictDetected. This is what gets put into event queues.

Write EventQueue as a type alias: asyncio.Queue[WorkflowEvent]. Users import this to annotate their own queues.

Write _Node as a frozen dataclass with fields: agent_id: str, fn: AgentFn, context_keys: tuple[str, ...]. Use tuple not list so the frozen dataclass is truly immutable — list elements can still be mutated even with frozen=True.

Write _Edge as a frozen dataclass with fields: from_agent: str, to_agent: str, condition: EdgeCondition.

Write the AgentGraph class:

__init__ takes: store: StateStore | None = None, max_concurrent: int = 10, conflict_resolver: ConflictResolver | None = None, invariant_checkers: list[InvariantChecker] | None = None. Creates self._store = store or InMemoryStore(). Creates self._nodes: dict[str, _Node] = {}. Creates self._edges: list[_Edge] = []. Creates self._sem = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent). Creates self._conflict_detector = ConflictDetector(conflict_resolver or LastWriteWins()). Creates self._invariant_checkers: list[InvariantChecker] = invariant_checkers or []. Class docstring must explain the round model: each call to run() executes in rounds; in each round all agents whose incoming edges fire run concurrently against the same state snapshot; their patches are batch-resolved for conflicts; winning patches are applied; invariants are checked; the next round is computed.

node(self, agent_id: str, context: list[str] | None = None) -> Callable[[AgentFn], AgentFn]: returns a decorator. The decorator takes a function fn: AgentFn, creates _Node(agent_id=agent_id, fn=fn, context_keys=tuple(context or [])), stores it in self._nodes[agent_id], and returns fn unchanged. Docstring must include a working example showing the decorator usage with context keys.

edge(self, from_agent: str, to_agent: str, condition: EdgeCondition | None = None) -> None: creates _Edge(from_agent=from_agent, to_agent=to_agent, condition=condition or (lambda _s: True)) and appends to self._edges. Docstring: "Add a directed edge from from_agent to to_agent. Multiple edges from the same source agent cause all reachable agents to be collected and run together in the next parallel round."

add_invariant(self, checker: InvariantChecker) -> None: appends to self._invariant_checkers. Docstring: "Register an additional invariant checker. Checkers run after every round completes. Error-severity violations halt the workflow with RuntimeError."

async def run(self, state: SharedState, start: str | list[str], event_queue: EventQueue | None = None) -> SharedState: the main execution method. Normalizes start to a list with start_ids = [start] if isinstance(start, str) else list(start). Validates all start agents are registered — raise ValueError with a helpful message listing registered agents if any are missing. Extracts workflow_id = state.workflow_id. Creates and emits WorkflowStarted. Resets self._conflict_detector. Sets current_state = state and current_round_ids = start_ids. Enters while loop: calls await self._execute_round(...) to get new state, computes current_state.model_dump() once and passes it to self._next_round(current_round_ids, state_dict) to get next round, updates current_round_ids. After loop: emits WorkflowCompleted. Returns current_state. Docstring explains Parameters, Returns, and Raises.

async def _execute_round(self, agent_ids: list[str], state: SharedState, workflow_id: str, event_queue: EventQueue | None) -> SharedState: runs one round. Step 1: calls await self._run_agents_parallel(agent_ids, state) to get patches. Step 2: calls self._conflict_detector.resolve_batch(patches) to get result. Step 3: for each conflict in result.conflicts, calls await self._emit(self._build_conflict_event(workflow_id, record), event_queue). Step 4: computes pre_round_dict = state.model_dump() once. Step 5: for each winner in result.winners — computes old_value = get_nested(pre_round_dict, winner.target), applies the patch with current_state = apply_patch(current_state, winner), computes new_value = get_nested(current_state.model_dump(), winner.target), emits a PatchApplied event. Step 6: runs invariant checks on the final state after all patches — calls check_all(current_state, self._invariant_checkers), if any error-severity violations exist raises RuntimeError with the descriptions joined by semicolons. Returns current_state.

async def _run_agents_parallel(self, agent_ids: list[str], state: SharedState) -> list[StatePatch]: defines an inner async def _call_one(agent_id: str) -> StatePatch coroutine. Inside _call_one: validate the agent is registered. Get the node. Compute context = slice_state(state, list(node.context_keys)). Acquire the semaphore with async with self._sem:. Call return await node.fn(context). Then at the outer level: patches = list(await asyncio.gather(*(_call_one(aid) for aid in agent_ids))). Return patches.

def _next_round(self, just_ran: list[str], state_dict: dict) -> list[str]: iterates self._edges. For each agent in just_ran, finds all edges where edge.from_agent == agent_id and edge.condition(state_dict) is True. Collects destination agent ids into a list, using a seen: set[str] to deduplicate. Returns the deduplicated list. If empty, the run is done.

async def _emit(self, event: WorkflowEvent, event_queue: EventQueue | None) -> None: calls await self._store.append(event) and if event_queue is not None calls event_queue.put_nowait(event).

@staticmethod def _build_conflict_event(workflow_id: str, record: ConflictRecord) -> ConflictDetected: constructs a ConflictDetected event from the record. Sets existing_patch = record.existing_patch and incoming_patch = record.incoming_patch — these are already dicts after the ConflictRecord fix.

docs.title: tests/unit/test_patch.py
docs.text:
Import pytest. Import StatePatch, set_nested, get_nested, apply_patch from agentstatelib.core.patch. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state.

Write six tests:

test_set_nested_simple — call set_nested({}, "a", "value"). Assert result equals {"a": "value"}.

test_set_nested_deep_path — call set_nested({}, "a.b.c", 42). Assert result["a"]["b"]["c"] == 42.

test_set_nested_creates_missing_keys — call set_nested({}, "tasks.t1.status", "done"). Assert no exception is raised and result["tasks"]["t1"]["status"] == "done".

test_get_nested_returns_none_for_missing — call get_nested({}, "a.b.c"). Assert result is None. No exception should be raised.

test_apply_patch_returns_new_object — create s = SharedState(goal="test"). Create p = StatePatch(agent_id="a", target="facts.key", value="val", reason="test"). Call new_s = apply_patch(s, p). Assert new_s is not s (different Python objects). Assert s.facts == {} (original unchanged). Assert new_s.facts.get("key") == "val".

test_apply_patch_deep_target — create state. Apply patch with target="tasks.t1.status" and value="done". Assert the new state has the value at that path — either as a dict key or as an attribute on a Task model depending on how Pydantic coerces nested dicts.

docs.title: tests/unit/test_context.py
docs.text:
Import pytest. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import StatePatch, apply_patch from agentstatelib.core.patch. Import slice_state from agentstatelib.router.context.

Write three tests:

test_slice_returns_only_requested_paths — create s = SharedState(goal="my goal"). Call result = slice_state(s, ["goal"]). Assert "goal" in result. Assert "tasks" not in result. Assert "artifacts" not in result.

test_slice_handles_nested_paths — create a state. Apply a patch setting "facts.key1" to "value1". Apply another setting "facts.key2" to "value2". Call result = slice_state(final_state, ["facts.key1"]). Assert result["facts"]["key1"] == "value1". Assert "key2" not in result.get("facts", {}).

test_empty_include_returns_full_state — create state. Call result = slice_state(state, []). Assert "goal" in result. Assert "tasks" in result. Assert "artifacts" in result.

docs.title: tests/integration/test_graph_run.py
docs.text:
Import pytest. Import asyncio. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch. Import AgentGraph from agentstatelib.router.graph. All tests marked with @pytest.mark.asyncio.

Write three tests. Use stub agent functions — plain async functions returning hardcoded StatePatch objects. No real model calls.

test_single_agent_workflow — define async def stub_agent(ctx): return StatePatch(agent_id="a", target="facts.done", value=True, reason="test"). Create graph = AgentGraph(). Register with @graph.node("agent_a")(stub_agent) or as a decorator. No edges. Call final = await graph.run(SharedState(goal="test"), start="agent_a"). Assert final.facts.get("done") is True.

test_two_agent_sequential_pipeline — define two agents: agent_a sets "facts.step" to 1, agent_b sets "facts.step" to 2. Register both. Add edge from "agent_a" to "agent_b" with condition lambda s: s.get("facts", {}).get("step") == 1. Run from "agent_a". Assert final.facts.get("step") == 2.

test_unknown_start_agent_raises — create empty AgentGraph(). Use pytest.raises(ValueError) and inside call await graph.run(SharedState(goal="x"), start="nonexistent").

docs.title: docs/concepts/patches.md
docs.text:
Write the Patches concept guide.

Section 1 — What a patch is. A proposal, not a write. Agents return a StatePatch describing what they want to change. The library decides whether to apply it. Explain why this layer exists: without it there is no way to detect when two agents want to change the same thing, no attribution for who changed what, no reason field for the audit log.

Section 2 — The patch pipeline. Draw the flow in text: agent returns StatePatch → ConflictDetector.resolve_batch() → apply_patch() → InvariantChecker.check() → new SharedState → PatchApplied event logged. Each step has a single responsibility.

Section 3 — The target field. Explain dotted paths with examples: "facts.domain", "tasks.research.status", "artifacts.draft.content". Show how missing intermediate keys are created automatically by set_nested.

Section 4 — The reason field. Explain this is not optional in spirit. The reason field is what makes the event log readable during debugging. "done" tells you nothing. "Completed literature review — found 12 relevant papers" tells you exactly what happened.

Section 5 — Priority. Explain the priority field and when to use it with PriorityBased resolution. Default is 0. Set higher values on patches from agents that should win conflicts.

docs.title: docs/quickstart.md — fill in completely
docs.text:
The most important doc page. Users land here first.

Install section: pip install agentstate-lib

Working example — a complete 25-line sequential script using the decorator pattern. Three agents: planner, researcher, writer. Each returns a StatePatch. Edges connect them with conditions. Use stub logic that does not require any API key so users can run it immediately. Test this script yourself before writing it into the docs.

Parallel agents section — show how to run two agents in parallel by passing a list to start: await graph.run(state, start=["analyst_a", "analyst_b"]).

What is happening — bullet list explaining SharedState (shared world), the decorator (registers the function), StatePatch (the proposal), edge condition (when to advance), parallel start (both in round 1).

Next steps — links to SharedState and Patches concept pages.

docs.title: Test commands for Week 2
docs.text:
pytest tests/ -v

mypy agentstatelib/ --strict

python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch, Artifact

graph = AgentGraph()

@graph.node('planner', context=['goal'])
async def planner(ctx):
    return StatePatch(agent_id='planner', target='facts.plan',
                      value='done', reason='planned')

@graph.node('researcher', context=['facts'])
async def researcher(ctx):
    return StatePatch(agent_id='researcher', target='artifacts.result',
                      value=Artifact(produced_by='researcher', artifact_type='result', content='found'),
                      reason='researched')

graph.edge('planner', 'researcher',
           lambda s: s.get('facts', {}).get('plan') == 'done')

async def main():
    state = SharedState(goal='test workflow')
    final = await graph.run(state, start='planner')
    assert final.facts.get('plan') == 'done'
    assert final.artifacts.get('result').content == 'found'
    print('quickstart: ok')

asyncio.run(main())
"

mkdocs build --strict
python -m build

Context
docs.text:
You already have working conflicts.py, invariants.py, and graph.py from the review and correction sessions. This week: fix the failing integration test, update all conflict unit tests for the batch API, write new parallel execution tests proving the round model, update exports, and ship the coordination docs.

The failing test used a sequential edge so both agents ran in separate rounds — conflicts only occur when two agents run in the same round. Fix it by using parallel start.

docs.title: tests/unit/test_conflicts.py (rewrite)
docs.text:
The primary API is now resolve_batch(patches) -> BatchResolutionResult. The submit() method returns a tuple (winner, conflict_or_none). Rewrite all tests to use the correct API.

Import pytest. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch. Import ConflictDetector, BatchResolutionResult, LastWriteWins, PriorityBased, RejectIncoming, ConflictRecord from agentstatelib.coordination.conflicts.

Write eight test functions:

test_resolve_batch_no_conflict — create detector = ConflictDetector(LastWriteWins()). Create two patches with different targets ("facts.x" and "facts.y"). Call result = detector.resolve_batch([patch_a, patch_b]). Assert len(result.winners) == 2. Assert result.conflicts == [].

test_resolve_batch_detects_collision — create two patches both targeting "facts.x" with different values. Call result = detector.resolve_batch([patch_a, patch_b]). Assert len(result.conflicts) == 1. Assert len(result.winners) == 1 — one winner per unique path.

test_resolve_batch_last_write_wins — create two patches targeting the same path. Set patch_a.timestamp = 1000.0 and patch_b.timestamp = 2000.0. Note: since timestamp is set by default_factory you may need to use model_copy(update={"timestamp": 1000.0}) or construct with explicit timestamp. Use LastWriteWins() resolver. Assert the winner is the patch with timestamp 2000.0 by checking result.winners[0].agent_id.

test_resolve_batch_priority_based — two patches same path, priorities 1 and 10. Create detector = ConflictDetector(PriorityBased()). Assert winner has priority 10.

test_resolve_batch_reject_incoming — two patches same path. detector = ConflictDetector(RejectIncoming()). Assert winner is the first patch by checking its agent_id.

test_resolve_batch_three_patches_same_path — three patches targeting the same path with ascending timestamps. Assert len(result.winners) == 1. Assert len(result.conflicts) == 2. Assert the winner is the patch with the latest timestamp.

test_submit_returns_tuple — call winner, conflict = detector.submit(patch) with no prior patch to that path. Assert winner is a StatePatch. Assert conflict is None — no conflict when there is nothing to conflict with.

test_conflicts_accumulate_across_batches — call resolve_batch twice in sequence, each time with two patches targeting the same path (different paths between the two calls). Assert len(detector.conflicts) == 2 after both calls. Then call detector.reset(). Assert len(detector.conflicts) == 0.

docs.title: tests/unit/test_invariants.py (additions)
docs.text:
Keep all existing passing tests. Add these two tests:

test_task_with_none_goal_id_is_not_a_violation — create state = SharedState(goal="test"). Apply a patch setting "tasks.t1" to a dict with {"description": "do something", "goal_id": None, "status": "pending"}. Call check_all(state, [TasksReferenceExistingGoals()]). Assert result is an empty list — ungrouped tasks are not violations.

test_invariant_handles_dict_tasks — create a state. Apply a patch that sets "tasks.t1" to a plain dict (not a Task model instance). Call check_all(state, [TasksReferenceExistingGoals(), CompletedGoalsHaveNoBlockingTasks()]). Assert no AttributeError is raised — both checkers must handle dict tasks via the _get_task_field helper.

docs.title: tests/integration/test_conflict_flow.py
docs.text:
Import pytest. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch. Import AgentGraph from agentstatelib.router.graph. Import ConflictDetected from agentstatelib.core.events. Import InMemoryStore from agentstatelib.memory.store. Import InvariantChecker, InvariantViolation from agentstatelib.coordination.

Write four tests, all marked @pytest.mark.asyncio:

test_conflict_detected_and_logged_in_store — register agent_a returning a patch to "facts.status" with value "done". Register agent_b returning a patch to "facts.status" with value "failed". Do NOT add any edge between them. Call final = await graph.run(state, start=["agent_a", "agent_b"]) — both agents run in round 1 simultaneously. Retrieve events = await store.get_workflow(state.workflow_id). Filter for ConflictDetected instances. Assert len(conflict_events) >= 1.

test_conflict_event_has_correct_fields — same setup. Get the first ConflictDetected event. Assert event.path == "facts.status". Assert event.winner_agent_id in ("agent_a", "agent_b"). Assert event.loser_agent_id in ("agent_a", "agent_b"). Assert event.winner_agent_id != event.loser_agent_id. Assert event.resolution_strategy == "LastWriteWins".

test_conflict_final_state_is_consistent — same setup. Assert final.facts["status"] in ("done", "failed") — exactly one value, not None, not both.

test_invariant_violation_halts_workflow — define an inner class AlwaysError implementing InvariantChecker with a check method that always returns [InvariantViolation(rule_name="AlwaysError", description="Always fails.", severity="error")]. Create graph with invariant_checkers=[AlwaysError()]. Register one agent. Use pytest.raises(RuntimeError) and inside call await graph.run(state, start="agent_a").

docs.title: tests/integration/test_parallel_execution.py
docs.text:
New file. These tests prove the parallel round model. Import everything needed.

Write seven tests, all @pytest.mark.asyncio:

test_two_agents_in_same_round — register agent_a setting "facts.result_a" to 1 and agent_b setting "facts.result_b" to 2. No edges. final = await graph.run(state, start=["agent_a", "agent_b"]). Retrieve events. Filter PatchApplied. Assert len(patch_events) == 2. Assert both "result_a" and "result_b" are in final.facts.

test_parallel_agents_read_same_snapshot — this verifies the critical consistency guarantee. Before the main run, apply an initial patch setting "facts.counter" to 42 using apply_patch(initial_state, setup_patch). Register two agents both declared with context=["facts.counter"]. Each agent reads ctx["facts"]["counter"] from context and returns a patch setting their own result key. Run both in parallel. Retrieve PatchApplied events. The old_value field on each patch event for the counter path (if you store it that way) or the context the agent received should both be 42. Design the agents to store what they read in their patch value so you can assert it: agent_a sets "facts.a_saw" to ctx.get("facts", {}).get("counter") and agent_b sets "facts.b_saw" similarly. Assert final.facts["a_saw"] == 42 and final.facts["b_saw"] == 42.

test_sequential_rounds_chain — register planner (sets "facts.plan" to "done"), researcher (sets "artifacts.sources" to ["s1"]), writer (sets "artifacts.draft" to "draft"). Edge planner→researcher fires when "plan" in s.get("facts", {}). Edge researcher→writer fires when "sources" in s.get("artifacts", {}). Run from planner. Retrieve PatchApplied events. Assert they arrive in order: planner's target="facts.plan" first, then researcher's target="artifacts.sources", then writer's target="artifacts.draft".

test_fan_out — register coordinator (sets "facts.started" to True), analyst_a (sets "facts.result_a" to 1), analyst_b (sets "facts.result_b" to 2). Add edges coordinator→analyst_a and coordinator→analyst_b both unconditional. Run from coordinator. Retrieve PatchApplied events. Assert coordinator's patch appears first. Assert both analyst patches appear in the events. Assert final state has all three facts.

test_fan_out_then_fan_in — register coordinator, analyst_a, analyst_b, aggregator. Edges: coordinator→analyst_a, coordinator→analyst_b (unconditional), analyst_a→aggregator (unconditional), analyst_b→aggregator (unconditional). Run from coordinator. Retrieve PatchApplied events in order. The coordinator's patch should appear first, then the two analyst patches (in either order), then the aggregator's patch last. Assert aggregator only ran once despite two edges pointing to it.

test_parallel_start_with_list — register agent_a and agent_b. Call await graph.run(state, start=["agent_a", "agent_b"]). Assert no ValueError. Retrieve events. Assert both agents' patches appear.

test_unknown_start_agent_raises — pytest.raises(ValueError) wrapping await graph.run(state, start="not_registered").

docs.title: docs/concepts/conflict.md
docs.text:
Write the conflict detection concept guide.

Section 1 — What a conflict is. Two agents in the same parallel round both returning patches targeting the same dotted state path. Contrast with LangGraph: in LangGraph parallel node updates are merged with dict.update() which silently overwrites — the second writer wins and there is no record of the collision. In agentstatelib the same situation produces a ConflictDetected event, the configured resolver picks the winner, and both patches are preserved in the event log.

Section 2 — When conflicts happen. Only within a round. If agent_a updates "facts.status" in round 1 and agent_b updates "facts.status" in round 2, that is not a conflict — agent_b is making a deliberate update to already-applied state. Conflicts are detected in resolve_batch() which processes all patches from a single round together.

Section 3 — The resolution pipeline. Step by step: patches arrive in resolve_batch() → path collision detected → resolver.resolve(existing, incoming) called → winner determined → ConflictDetected event emitted → winning patch goes to apply_patch() → PatchApplied event emitted.

Section 4 — Resolution strategies. Explain LastWriteWins (default, newer timestamp wins, ties go to incoming), PriorityBased (set the priority field on StatePatch, higher value wins, ties fall back to last-write-wins), RejectIncoming (first patch to a path wins, incoming is logged but not applied — useful when you want the first agent to lock a path for a round). When to use each.

Section 5 — Custom resolver. Explain the ConflictResolver protocol: one method resolve(existing, incoming) -> StatePatch. Show a five-line pattern for writing a custom resolver. Note that any class with this method satisfies the protocol without importing anything from agentstatelib.

Section 6 — Reading the conflict log. Show how to retrieve events from the store, filter for ConflictDetected instances, and read path, winner_agent_id, loser_agent_id, resolution_strategy.

docs.title: docs/concepts/parallelism.md
docs.text:
Write the parallel execution concept guide.

Section 1 — The round model. Every call to graph.run() executes in rounds. In each round: all agents whose incoming edges fire are collected, all run concurrently against the same state snapshot, patches are resolved, winning patches are applied, invariants are checked, the next round is computed. No agent in a round sees any other agent's updates until the next round.

Section 2 — Why this matters. Eliminates read-write races within a round. All agents see identical state regardless of execution order. Conflicts are explicit and logged, not silent.

Section 3 — Fan-out topology. coordinator→[analyst_a, analyst_b]. Both analysts run in round 2 simultaneously. Show the two edge declarations that produce this. Explain that the coordinator's state update is visible to both analysts since it was applied at the end of round 1 before round 2 begins.

Section 4 — Fan-in. [analyst_a, analyst_b]→aggregator. Aggregator runs in round 3 after both analysts complete. Explain how _next_round deduplicates — the aggregator appears in the next round's list only once even though two edges point to it.

Section 5 — Parallel start. run(state, start=["agent_a", "agent_b"]) runs multiple agents in round 1 simultaneously.

Section 6 — The semaphore. max_concurrent in AgentGraph.__init__ controls an asyncio.Semaphore that limits total simultaneous agent coroutines across all rounds. Default is 10. Set it lower if your agents make API calls and you need to stay within rate limits.

docs.title: docs/api-reference/conflicts.md
docs.text:
Write the reference page using mkdocstrings autodoc directives. One heading and directive per class/function. Cover in this order: ConflictDetector, BatchResolutionResult, ConflictRecord, ConflictResolver, LastWriteWins, PriorityBased, RejectIncoming, InvariantChecker, InvariantViolation, check_all, TasksReferenceExistingGoals, CompletedGoalsHaveNoBlockingTasks.

Add a brief intro paragraph: "The coordination module contains two subsystems: conflict resolution (what happens when parallel agents target the same state path) and invariant checking (what happens after every round to verify state is still internally consistent)."

docs.title: Test commands for Week 3
docs.text:
pytest tests/ -v

mypy agentstatelib/ --strict

ruff check agentstatelib/

python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.core.events import ConflictDetected
from agentstatelib.memory.store import InMemoryStore

async def main():
    store = InMemoryStore()
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)

    @graph.node('analyst_a')
    async def analyst_a(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_a', target='facts.verdict',
                         value='positive', reason='a says positive')

    @graph.node('analyst_b')
    async def analyst_b(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_b', target='facts.verdict',
                         value='negative', reason='b says negative')

    state = SharedState(goal='conflict test')
    final = await graph.run(state, start=['analyst_a', 'analyst_b'])
    events = await store.get_workflow(state.workflow_id)
    conflicts = [e for e in events if isinstance(e, ConflictDetected)]
    print(f'Conflicts: {len(conflicts)}')
    print(f'Winner: {conflicts[0].winner_agent_id}')
    print(f'Final verdict: {final.facts[\"verdict\"]}')
    assert len(conflicts) == 1
    print('parallel conflict test: ok')

asyncio.run(main())
"

python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.core.events import PatchApplied
from agentstatelib.memory.store import InMemoryStore

async def main():
    store = InMemoryStore()
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)

    @graph.node('coordinator')
    async def coordinator(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='coordinator', target='facts.started',
                         value=True, reason='kicked off')

    @graph.node('analyst_a')
    async def analyst_a(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_a', target='facts.result_a',
                         value=1, reason='a done')

    @graph.node('analyst_b')
    async def analyst_b(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_b', target='facts.result_b',
                         value=2, reason='b done')

    graph.edge('coordinator', 'analyst_a')
    graph.edge('coordinator', 'analyst_b')

    state = SharedState(goal='fan-out test')
    final = await graph.run(state, start='coordinator')
    events = await store.get_workflow(state.workflow_id)
    patches = [e for e in events if isinstance(e, PatchApplied)]
    agents_ran = [p.agent_id for p in patches]
    print('Agents that ran:', agents_ran)
    assert 'coordinator' in agents_ran
    assert 'analyst_a' in agents_ran
    assert 'analyst_b' in agents_ran
    print('fan-out test: ok')

asyncio.run(main())
"

mkdocs build --strict
