docs.title: Phase 3 · Week 4 · FastAPI HTTP Layer · API Keys · List Endpoint · Dashboard Placeholder · v0.2
Context
docs.text:
This week you build the HTTP transport layer. Two important clarifications before writing any code:

API key model — self-hosted, not centrally issued. The x-api-key system authenticates callers to the developer's own server. A developer installs your library, starts the server, and sets AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS=their-own-secret in their environment. They configured that key themselves. There is no registration, no account, no key issuance from you. It works exactly like a database password — you set your own. A LangSmith-style hosted platform where you issue keys to signed-up users is the post-v1.0 startup product, not part of this week.

Web dashboard foundation starts here. You add a GET /v1/workflows list endpoint and a list_workflows() method to the StateStore protocol. You also add a GET /dashboard route that returns a placeholder HTML page. The full web dashboard UI ships in week 8. This week you wire the plumbing it will need.

docs.title: Files to work in
docs.text:
1. agentstatelib/memory/store.py (add list_workflows to protocol and both stores)
  2. agentstatelib/api/app.py
  3. agentstatelib/api/auth.py
  4. agentstatelib/api/routes.py
  5. agentstatelib/api/streaming.py
  6. agentstatelib/api/__init__.py
  7. tests/integration/test_api.py
  8. docs/guides/http-api.md
uv add --optional api fastapi "uvicorn[standard]"
uv add --dev httpx

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/store.py (update — add list_workflows)
docs.text:
Add a fifth method to the StateStore protocol. Below the existing four method stubs, add async def list_workflows(self) -> list[str] with body ... and docstring: "Return all unique workflow_ids in the store, most recent first."

Add the implementation to InMemoryStore. The method returns list(reversed(list(self._events.keys()))). Since self._events is an insertion-ordered dict in Python 3.7+, reversing it gives the most recently created workflow first.

Add the implementation to SQLiteStore. Open a connection with async with aiosqlite.connect(self.path) as db. Call await self._init_db(db). Execute the query SELECT workflow_id FROM events GROUP BY workflow_id ORDER BY MAX(id) DESC. Fetch all rows. Return [row[0] for row in rows]. Using MAX(id) in the ORDER BY ensures the workflow with the most recent event appears first.

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/app.py
docs.text:
Import FastAPI from fastapi. Import HTMLResponse from fastapi.responses. Import SQLiteStore from agentstatelib.memory.store.

Write create_app(db_path: str = "agentstatelib.db") -> FastAPI. Inside: create a FastAPI instance with title="agentstatelib API", version="0.2.0", and description="Self-hosted coordination layer for parallel multi-agent AI systems". Create SQLiteStore(db_path). Store it on app.state.store. Import router from agentstatelib.api.routes and call app.include_router(router, prefix="/v1"). Add a route @app.get("/dashboard", response_class=HTMLResponse, include_in_schema=False) that returns HTMLResponse("<html><body><h1>agentstatelib dashboard</h1><p>Full dashboard coming in v0.4.0. <a href='/docs'>API docs →</a></p></body></html>"). This placeholder keeps the route alive so the URL works in week 8 when you replace the body. Return app.

Write module-level app = create_app() so uvicorn can be invoked as uvicorn agentstatelib.api.app:app.

Docstring on create_app: "Create and configure the agentstatelib FastAPI application. Pass a custom db_path using pytest's tmp_path fixture in tests to get an isolated database per test. The server uses self-configured API keys via the AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS environment variable — there is no central key registry."

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/auth.py
docs.text:
Import Header, HTTPException from fastapi. Import os.

Write verify_api_key(x_api_key: str = Header(...)) -> str. Read valid keys from os.environ.get("AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS", "dev-key-123"). Split on ",". Strip whitespace from each entry. Collect into a set. If the provided key is not in that set: raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail={"error_code": "invalid_api_key", "message": "Invalid or missing API key. Set valid keys via the AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS environment variable as a comma-separated list."}). Return the key if valid.

Docstring: "FastAPI dependency for API key authentication. Inject with Depends(verify_api_key) on any protected endpoint. Keys are self-configured by whoever runs the server — set the AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS environment variable. There is no central key issuance. Generate a secure key with: python -c \"import secrets; print(secrets.token_urlsafe(32))\""

Write a separate helper generate_key() -> str that calls import secrets; return secrets.token_urlsafe(32). This is not used internally but is importable by developers who want to generate keys programmatically.

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/routes.py
docs.text:
Import APIRouter, HTTPException, Depends, Request, Query from fastapi. Import StreamingResponse, HTMLResponse from fastapi.responses. Import asyncio. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch. Import WorkflowStarted, PatchApplied from agentstatelib.core.events. Import verify_api_key from agentstatelib.api.auth. Import stream_workflow_events from agentstatelib.api.streaming.

Write get_store(request: Request) — dependency that returns request.app.state.store. Docstring: "FastAPI dependency that returns the shared StateStore from app.state."

Write a Pydantic model CreateWorkflowRequest with fields goal: str and workflow_type: str = "general". This is the request body for POST /workflows.

Write router = APIRouter() at module level. Define these seven endpoints:

@router.get("/health", tags=["system"]) — no auth, no store dependency. Returns {"status": "ok", "version": "0.2.0"}. Docstring: "Health check. Returns immediately with no side effects. No authentication required. Used by load balancers and monitoring systems."

@router.get("/workflows", tags=["workflows"]) — requires Depends(verify_api_key) and Depends(get_store). Calls workflow_ids = await store.list_workflows(). Returns {"workflow_ids": workflow_ids, "count": len(workflow_ids)}. Docstring: "List all workflow IDs in the store, most recent first."

@router.post("/workflows", status_code=201, tags=["workflows"]) — auth and store deps. Request body is CreateWorkflowRequest. Creates state = SharedState(goal=body.goal, workflow_type=body.workflow_type). Creates event = WorkflowStarted(workflow_id=state.workflow_id, agent_id="system", workflow_type=body.workflow_type, goal=body.goal). Calls await store.append(event). Returns state. Docstring: "Create a new workflow. Returns the initial SharedState with a generated workflow_id."

@router.get("/workflows/{workflow_id}", tags=["workflows"]) — auth and store deps. Gets events = await store.get_workflow(workflow_id). If not events: raises HTTPException(404, detail={"error_code": "workflow_not_found", "message": f"No events found for workflow {workflow_id!r}"}). Imports replay from agentstatelib.memory.replay inside the function body (avoids circular import at module load, gracefully errors at runtime before week 5). Returns replay(events). Docstring: "Get the current reconstructed state of a workflow by replaying its event log."

@router.post("/workflows/{workflow_id}/patches", tags=["workflows"]) — auth and store deps. Request body is StatePatch. Gets events to verify workflow exists (404 if not). Creates patch_event = PatchApplied(workflow_id=workflow_id, agent_id=patch.agent_id, patch_id=patch.patch_id, target=patch.target, old_value=None, new_value=patch.value, reason=patch.reason, timestamp=patch.timestamp). Note: old_value is None here because we do not replay state just to compute it — the HTTP patch endpoint is for external submissions. Calls await store.append(patch_event). Returns the replayed state. Docstring: "Submit a patch to a workflow outside of a graph run. Useful for human-in-the-loop scenarios where a person reviews and approves a state change before the workflow continues."

@router.get("/workflows/{workflow_id}/events", tags=["workflows"]) — requires both auth via header AND accepts key: str | None = Query(None) as a query parameter fallback. This dual authentication is required because the browser EventSource API cannot set custom headers — the key must be passable as a URL query param. Authentication logic: if x_api_key header is present and valid, proceed; else if key query param is present and valid, proceed; else raise 401. Returns StreamingResponse(stream_workflow_events(store, workflow_id), media_type="text/event-stream", headers={"Cache-Control": "no-cache", "X-Accel-Buffering": "no"}). The X-Accel-Buffering: no header disables buffering in Nginx and other reverse proxies which would otherwise hold back SSE events. Docstring: "Stream workflow events as Server-Sent Events. Accepts authentication via x-api-key header or key query parameter (e.g. ?key=yourkey). The query parameter is required for browser EventSource connections."

@router.get("/workflows/{workflow_id}/events-list", tags=["workflows"]) — auth and store deps. Gets all events. Returns {"events": [e.model_dump() for e in events], "count": len(events)}. Docstring: "Get all events for a workflow as a JSON array. Used by the web dashboard to load workflow history without a streaming connection."

Every error response across all endpoints uses a dict with error_code and message keys. Never plain string errors. Add a module-level comment explaining this convention.

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/streaming.py
docs.text:
Import asyncio. Import StateStore from agentstatelib.memory.store.

Write async def stream_workflow_events(store: StateStore, workflow_id: str, poll_interval: float = 0.5) as an async generator function using yield. At the very start, before the loop, yield "retry: 3000 " — this single line tells SSE clients to reconnect after 3 seconds if the connection drops. Then initialize last_count = 0. Loop for _ in range(600) (5 minutes at 0.5s poll). In each iteration: call new_events = await store.since(workflow_id, last_count). For each event in new_events: yield f"data: {event.model_dump_json()} ". After the loop: update last_count += len(new_events). Then await asyncio.sleep(poll_interval).

Docstring: "Async generator yielding Server-Sent Events for a workflow. Emits all existing events on connect, then polls every poll_interval seconds for new events. Maximum stream duration is 5 minutes (600 × 0.5s). Consumed by GET /v1/workflows/{id}/events. The retry directive at the start tells clients to reconnect automatically on disconnection."

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/__init__.py
docs.text: Import create_app from agentstatelib.api.app. Set __all__ = ["create_app"].

docs.title: tests/integration/test_api.py
docs.text:
Import pytest. Import TestClient from fastapi.testclient. Import create_app from agentstatelib.api.

Write a pytest fixture client that takes tmp_path and returns TestClient(create_app(db_path=str(tmp_path / "test.db"))). Use tmp_path for an isolated real file — aiosqlite's async context manager may not behave reliably with :memory:. The fixture does not need yield since TestClient cleans up itself.

Write seven synchronous tests (TestClient handles async internally):

test_health_no_auth_required — GET /health with no headers. Assert response.status_code == 200. Assert response.json()["status"] == "ok". Assert "version" in response.json().

test_create_workflow_requires_auth — POST /v1/workflows with JSON body {"goal": "test"} and no auth header. Assert response.status_code == 401. Assert response.json()["detail"]["error_code"] == "invalid_api_key".

test_create_workflow — POST with header {"x-api-key": "dev-key-123"} and body {"goal": "test goal", "workflow_type": "research"}. Assert response.status_code == 201. Assert "workflow_id" in response.json(). Assert response.json()["goal"] == "test goal".

test_create_and_retrieve_workflow — create a workflow. Take workflow_id = response.json()["workflow_id"]. GET /v1/workflows/{workflow_id} with auth. Assert 200. Assert response.json()["workflow_id"] == workflow_id.

test_list_workflows — create two workflows via POST. GET /v1/workflows with auth. Assert 200. Assert response.json()["count"] == 2. Assert both workflow_ids appear in response.json()["workflow_ids"].

test_patch_updates_state — create a workflow. POST /v1/workflows/{id}/patches with auth and body containing a StatePatch-shaped dict with agent_id="test", target="facts.test_key", value="hello", reason="test patch". GET the workflow. Assert response.json()["facts"]["test_key"] == "hello".

test_get_unknown_workflow_returns_404 — GET /v1/workflows/nonexistent-workflow-id with auth. Assert response.status_code == 404. Assert response.json()["detail"]["error_code"] == "workflow_not_found".

docs.title: docs/guides/http-api.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — When to use the HTTP API. Use it when agents run in separate processes, when external systems need to query or update workflow state, when streaming live events to a browser-based dashboard, or when building human-in-the-loop workflows where a person submits a patch to advance state. Use the Python library directly when everything runs in one process.

Section 2 — API key model. Explain this clearly and specifically: the x-api-key header authenticates callers to your own server. You configure valid keys by setting the AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS environment variable to a comma-separated list before starting the server. There is no sign-up, no central registry, no keys issued by agentstatelib. It is like a database password — you set your own. Show how to generate a secure key: python -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_urlsafe(32))". Show the environment variable pattern: AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS=key1,key2,key3 supports multiple keys so you can rotate without downtime.

Section 3 — Running the server. Show: $env:AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS = "your-key-here" then uvicorn agentstatelib.api.app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000. Show the health check curl. Mention the auto-generated OpenAPI docs at /docs.

Section 4 — Endpoint reference. Show curl commands for all seven endpoints including the new list and events-list endpoints. For the SSE endpoint show both the header form (for curl and httpx) and the query param form (for browser EventSource). Show a Python httpx async example connecting to the SSE stream.

Section 5 — SSE streaming. Explain Server-Sent Events. Show the browser-side JavaScript EventSource pattern using the query param auth: new EventSource('/v1/workflows/{id}/events?key=yourkey'). Explain the Cache-Control: no-cache and X-Accel-Buffering: no headers and why they matter when running behind Nginx or a cloud load balancer.

Section 6 — Human-in-the-loop workflows. Show the pattern: run a graph up to a checkpoint, surface the state to a human via GET, let the human submit a patch via POST to /patches, then resume the graph from that state. This is the use case that makes the HTTP API worth having.

docs.title: Test and run commands for Week 4
docs.text:
pytest tests/integration/test_api.py -v

mypy agentstatelib/ --strict

# Generate a real key
python -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_urlsafe(32))"

# Start the server
$env:AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS = "dev-key-123"
uvicorn agentstatelib.api.app:app --reload

# In another terminal — test all endpoints
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/health

curl -H "x-api-key: dev-key-123" http://localhost:8000/v1/workflows

curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/v1/workflows `
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
  -H "x-api-key: dev-key-123" `
  -d '{"goal": "test workflow", "workflow_type": "research"}'

# Replace {id} with the workflow_id from the previous response
curl -H "x-api-key: dev-key-123" http://localhost:8000/v1/workflows/{id}

# Open auto-generated API docs
start http://localhost:8000/docs

# Open dashboard placeholder
start "http://localhost:8000/dashboard"

mkdocs build --strict

docs.title: Done when
docs.text: All seven API tests pass. Server starts. Health returns 200. Creating, listing, and retrieving workflows all work correctly. Dashboard route returns the placeholder page. Unknown workflow returns 404 with structured error. Docs build cleanly.

docs.title: Phase 3 · Week 5 · Persistence · Checkpoint · Replay · v0.3
Context
docs.text: The replay debugger is your most visible competitive differentiator. When a workflow produces a wrong result, you step through the event log event by event and inspect state at each point. LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen discard intermediate state once a run completes. You keep everything.

docs.title: Files to work in
docs.text:
1. agentstatelib/memory/store.py (add PostgreSQL stub)
  2. agentstatelib/memory/checkpoint.py
  3. agentstatelib/memory/replay.py
  4. agentstatelib/memory/__init__.py (update exports)
  5. agentstatelib/__init__.py (update version and exports)

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/store.py (add PostgreSQL stub)
docs.text:
At the top of the file, after existing imports, add: try: import asyncpg; HAS_ASYNCPG = True on one line and except ImportError: asyncpg = None; HAS_ASYNCPG = False on the next.

Write PostgreSQLStore class. __init__ takes dsn: str. If HAS_ASYNCPG is False: raise ImportError("asyncpg required for PostgreSQLStore. Install with: pip install asyncpg"). Store self.dsn = dsn. Set self._pool = None.

Write private async def _get_pool(self). If self._pool is None: set self._pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(self.dsn). Return self._pool.

Write private async def _init_db(self). Gets pool. Executes in a transaction: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS events (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, event_id TEXT NOT NULL, workflow_id TEXT NOT NULL, type TEXT NOT NULL, data TEXT NOT NULL, timestamp DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL). Then CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_wf_id ON events(workflow_id).

Implement append: get pool, execute INSERT INTO events (event_id, workflow_id, type, data, timestamp) VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4, $5) using asyncpg's $N placeholder syntax.

Implement get_workflow: execute SELECT data FROM events WHERE workflow_id=$1 ORDER BY id ASC, fetch all, return deserialized list.

Implement since: same query with OFFSET $2 added.

Implement count: execute SELECT COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE workflow_id=$1, fetch one row, return the integer.

Implement list_workflows: execute SELECT DISTINCT workflow_id FROM events ORDER BY MAX(id) DESC, return list of strings. This requires a GROUP BY since DISTINCT with ORDER BY MAX needs it: SELECT workflow_id FROM events GROUP BY workflow_id ORDER BY MAX(id) DESC.

Add async def close(self) -> None that calls await self._pool.close() if pool is not None.

Docstring: "PostgreSQL-backed StateStore for production multi-process deployments. Requires asyncpg: pip install asyncpg. DSN format: postgresql://user:password@host/database. Connection pool is created lazily on first use. Call close() when done."

Add to tests/unit/test_store.py:

test_both_stores_satisfy_protocol — assert isinstance(InMemoryStore(), StateStore) is True. assert isinstance(SQLiteStore(":memory:"), StateStore) is True. If either fails, @runtime_checkable is broken.

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/checkpoint.py
docs.text:
Import BaseModel, Field from pydantic. Import Path from pathlib. Import uuid, time. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import StateStore from agentstatelib.memory.store.

Write Checkpoint model with fields: checkpoint_id (str, default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4())), workflow_id (str), state (SharedState), event_count (int — how many events existed in the store when this was saved), created_at (float, time factory). Docstring: "A snapshot of SharedState at a point in time. event_count marks where in the event log the workflow had progressed, so recovery can resume from the store without replaying the entire history from scratch."

Write async def save_checkpoint(state: SharedState, store: StateStore, directory: str = ".checkpoints") -> Checkpoint. Call Path(directory).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True). Call count = await store.count(state.workflow_id). Create Checkpoint(workflow_id=state.workflow_id, state=state, event_count=count). Construct path as Path(directory) / f"{state.workflow_id}_{checkpoint.checkpoint_id}.json". Call path.write_text(checkpoint.model_dump_json()). Return checkpoint.

Write def load_latest_checkpoint(workflow_id: str, directory: str = ".checkpoints") -> Checkpoint | None. Call Path(directory).glob(f"{workflow_id}_*.json") and convert to list. If empty return None. Sort by f.stat().st_mtime descending. Return Checkpoint.model_validate_json(files[0].read_text()).

Write def load_checkpoint(checkpoint_id: str, directory: str = ".checkpoints") -> Checkpoint. Call Path(directory).glob(f"*_{checkpoint_id}.json") and convert to list. If empty: raise FileNotFoundError(f"Checkpoint {checkpoint_id!r} not found in {directory!r}"). Return Checkpoint.model_validate_json(matches[0].read_text()).

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/replay.py
docs.text:
Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import StateEvent, WorkflowStarted, PatchApplied from agentstatelib.core.events. Import StatePatch, apply_patch from agentstatelib.core.patch.

Write replay(events: list[StateEvent]) -> SharedState. Iterate to find the first WorkflowStarted event using isinstance(e, WorkflowStarted). If none found: raise ValueError("Cannot replay: no WorkflowStarted event found in event log"). Extract workflow_id, workflow_type, goal from it. Create current_state = SharedState(workflow_id=ws_event.workflow_id, workflow_type=ws_event.workflow_type, goal=ws_event.goal). Sort all events by timestamp ascending. For each event in sorted order: if isinstance(event, PatchApplied), construct a StatePatch(agent_id=event.agent_id, target=event.target, value=event.new_value, reason=event.reason, patch_id=event.patch_id) and call current_state = apply_patch(current_state, patch). Return current_state. Docstring: "Reconstruct SharedState from an event log by finding the WorkflowStarted event for metadata then applying all PatchApplied events in timestamp order. Pass a truncated list to reconstruct any past state."

Write ReplayDebugger. __init__ takes events: list[StateEvent]. Sort by timestamp ascending and store as self._events. Set self._cursor = 0. Class docstring: "Step through a workflow's event log to inspect state at any point. When a workflow produces unexpected results, use this to find the exact moment state diverged. No equivalent tool exists in LangGraph, CrewAI, or AutoGen."

step(self) -> tuple[StateEvent, SharedState] — if self._cursor >= len(self._events): raise StopIteration("No more events to replay"). Increment self._cursor. Return (self._events[self._cursor - 1], replay(self._events[:self._cursor])).

jump_to(self, index: int) -> SharedState — if index < 0 or index > len(self._events): raise IndexError(f"Index {index} out of range [0, {len(self._events)}]"). Set self._cursor = index. Return replay(self._events[:index]).

state_at(self, timestamp: float) -> SharedState — filter events where e.timestamp <= timestamp. Return replay(filtered). Docstring: "Reconstruct state as it was at the given timestamp. Useful for finding the exact state at the moment a conflict or error occurred."

reset(self) -> None — sets self._cursor = 0.

current_index property — returns self._cursor.

total_events property — returns len(self._events).

docs.title: tests/unit/test_replay.py
docs.text:
Write five tests:

test_replay_reconstructs_state — create WorkflowStarted event and two PatchApplied events setting facts.a=1 and facts.b=2. Call state = replay(events). Assert state.facts.get("a") == 1 and state.facts.get("b") == 2.

test_replay_missing_workflow_started_raises — call replay([patch_applied_event_with_no_workflow_started]). Assert pytest.raises(ValueError).

test_debugger_step_advances_cursor — create debugger with 3 events. Call step() twice. Assert debugger.current_index == 2.

test_debugger_jump_to_index — create debugger with 5 events. Call jump_to(3). Assert returned state reflects only first 3 events.

test_debugger_stop_iteration_at_end — create debugger with 1 event. Call step() once. Assert second step() raises StopIteration via pytest.raises.

docs.title: tests/integration/test_checkpoint_recovery.py
docs.text:
Write three tests, all @pytest.mark.asyncio:

test_save_and_load_checkpoint — run one-agent workflow with SQLiteStore(str(tmp_path / "test.db")). Call cp = await save_checkpoint(final, store). Call loaded = load_latest_checkpoint(final.workflow_id). Assert loaded is not None. Assert loaded.state.workflow_id == final.workflow_id. Assert loaded.event_count > 0. Clean up .checkpoints directory after test with shutil.rmtree(".checkpoints", ignore_errors=True).

test_checkpoint_preserves_full_state — run workflow setting several facts and producing one artifact. Save checkpoint. Load it. Assert loaded.state.facts == final.facts. Assert set(loaded.state.artifacts.keys()) == set(final.artifacts.keys()).

test_replay_debugger_full_walkthrough — run two-agent sequential workflow. Get all events. Create ReplayDebugger(events). Step through with a while loop catching StopIteration. Count steps. Assert step count equals len(events). Assert the final state from the last step has facts matching the final state from the graph run.

docs.title: docs/concepts/event-log.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — What the event log is. Append-only sequence of typed events. Every state change, conflict, checkpoint, and error produces one. Events are immutable.

Section 2 — State as a projection. SharedState is derived from events, never stored directly. current_state = replay(all_events). This is event sourcing from distributed systems applied to AI workflow coordination.

Section 3 — What you get for free. Audit trail (every change has agent attribution and a reason), replay (reconstruct any past state), conflict history (ConflictDetected events preserve both patches), recovery (checkpoints + event log for fault tolerance).

Section 4 — How to read the log. Show store.get_workflow(workflow_id) and filtering by isinstance. Show reading the reason field from PatchApplied events to understand what each agent was doing.

docs.title: docs/guides/checkpoint-recovery.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — When to use checkpoints. Long workflows where failure near the end is expensive, workflows calling paid APIs, workflows on unreliable hardware.

Section 2 — Saving a checkpoint. Show the call. Explain: full SharedState stored to disk, event_count marks progress in the store.

Section 3 — Recovering. Show load_latest_checkpoint. Explain that event_count tells you where the workflow was — you resume agents from that point without replaying everything.

docs.title: docs/guides/replay-debugger.md
docs.text: Frame around a debugging story. Problem: workflow ran but produced the wrong answer. Steps: get events, create ReplayDebugger(events), step loop, find the moment the wrong value appeared, examine that event. Show state_at(timestamp) for jumping to a specific conflict. Closing: "In LangGraph, once a run completes intermediate states are gone. In agentstatelib every intermediate state is recoverable."

docs.title: Test and publish commands for Week 5
docs.text:
pytest tests/ -v
mypy agentstatelib/ --strict

python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.memory.store import SQLiteStore
from agentstatelib.memory.checkpoint import save_checkpoint, load_latest_checkpoint
from agentstatelib.memory.replay import ReplayDebugger

async def main():
    store = SQLiteStore('ckpt_test.db')
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)
    state = SharedState(goal='checkpoint test')

    @graph.node('agent_a')
    async def agent_a(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='agent_a', target='facts.step',
                         value=1, reason='step 1')

    final = await graph.run(state, start='agent_a')
    cp = await save_checkpoint(final, store)
    print('Saved:', cp.checkpoint_id)
    loaded = load_latest_checkpoint(final.workflow_id)
    print('Loaded facts:', loaded.state.facts)
    events = await store.get_workflow(final.workflow_id)
    debugger = ReplayDebugger(events)
    n = 0
    while True:
        try:
            event, s = debugger.step()
            print(f'  Step {n}: {event.type}')
            n += 1
        except StopIteration:
            break
    print(f'Replayed {n} events')

asyncio.run(main())
"

Remove-Item ckpt_test.db -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Remove-Item -Recurse .checkpoints -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

mkdocs build --strict
python -m build
twine upload dist/*
git tag v0.3.0
git push origin v0.3.0
mike deploy --push --update-aliases 0.3 latest

docs.title: agentstatelib/contrib/base_agent.py
docs.text:
Module docstring: "Optional base class for LLM-backed agent functions. Provides the retry-with-correction loop common across all providers. Not required — any async callable accepting a dict and returning a StatePatch is a valid AgentFn."

Import json from stdlib. Import Any from typing. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch.

Write MaxRetriesExceeded(Exception). __init__ stores: self.agent_id = agent_id, self.attempts = attempts, self.last_error = last_error, self.last_output = last_output. Call super().__init__(str(self)). __str__ returns a multi-line string showing all four fields so the developer immediately sees what the model returned and what error it caused.

Write LLMAgent class with class docstring that includes a complete working example — the subclass pattern showing how to implement _call_model and register with the graph.

__init__ stores: self.agent_id, self.system_prompt, self.model, self.max_retries = max_retries where max_retries: int = 3.

async def __call__(self, context: dict) -> StatePatch — the retry loop. Initialize last_error: str | None = None and last_output: str | None = None. For _ in range(self.max_retries): call raw = await self._call_model(self._build_prompt(context, error=last_error)). Strip markdown: cleaned = raw.strip().removeprefix("```json").removeprefix("```").removesuffix("```").strip(). Try parsed = json.loads(cleaned) — on json.JSONDecodeError as e: last_error = str(e), last_output = raw, continue. Try return StatePatch(agent_id=self.agent_id, **parsed) — on any Exception as e: last_error = str(e), last_output = raw, continue. After loop: raise MaxRetriesExceeded(agent_id=self.agent_id, attempts=self.max_retries, last_error=last_error or "unknown", last_output=last_output or "").

def _build_prompt(self, context: dict, error: str | None = None) -> str — joins four parts with " ": (1) self.system_prompt, (2) "Current state context: " + json.dumps(context, indent=2, default=str), (3) the fixed JSON instruction block: "Return a JSON object with exactly these three fields: - target: string (the dotted state path to update) - value: any valid JSON (the new value) - reason: string (one sentence explaining this update) Return only the JSON object. No markdown. No explanation. No code fences.", (4) if error is not None: f"Your previous response failed with this error: {error} Return corrected JSON only.".

async def _call_model(self, prompt: str) -> str — body is raise NotImplementedError("Subclass must implement _call_model"). Docstring: "Make one call to your model with the given prompt. Return the raw string response. The base class handles JSON parsing, StatePatch validation, and the retry loop."

docs.title: examples/models/openai_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment block at top:

# Reference implementation — copy and adapt for your project.
# NOT part of the agentstatelib package.
# Works with: OpenAI API, Azure OpenAI, vLLM (OpenAI-compatible),
#             Together AI, Fireworks AI, Groq (openai-compatible endpoint).

Import httpx, json, os, asyncio. Import load_dotenv from dotenv. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib. Import LLMAgent from agentstatelib.contrib.base_agent.

Write OpenAIAgent(LLMAgent). Additional __init__ params: api_key: str | None = None (defaults to os.environ.get("OPENAI_API_KEY")), base_url: str = "https://api.openai.com/v1".

_call_model: async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=60.0) as client:. POST to f"{self.base_url}/chat/completions". Headers: {"Authorization": f"Bearer {self.api_key}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}. Body: {"model": self.model, "messages": [{"role": "system", "content": self.system_prompt}, {"role": "user", "content": prompt}], "response_format": {"type": "json_object"}}. Call response.raise_for_status(). Return response.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"].

Write async def main() loading dotenv, creating an agent, calling it with a sample context, printing the StatePatch and elapsed time.

docs.title: examples/models/anthropic_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment block noting differences from OpenAI.

Write AnthropicAgent(LLMAgent). api_key from ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var.

_call_model: endpoint https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages. Headers: {"x-api-key": self.api_key, "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01", "Content-Type": "application/json"}. Body: {"model": self.model, "max_tokens": 1000, "system": self.system_prompt, "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]}. Response content at response.json()["content"][0]["text"]. Add comment: "Anthropic has no native JSON mode. The prompt instruction in _build_prompt handles this. The retry loop handles cases where the model adds explanation before the JSON."

docs.title: examples/models/ollama_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment block:

# Reference implementation for Ollama.
# Works with any Ollama instance — local, Docker, remote server.
# Set base_url to point at your Ollama server.
# The library does not know or care where your Ollama runs.

Write OllamaAgent(LLMAgent). Additional params: base_url: str defaulting to os.environ.get("OLLAMA_BASE_URL", "http://localhost:11434"), use_grammar: bool = True.

_call_model: async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=120.0) as client:. POST to f"{self.base_url}/api/generate". Body dict: {"model": self.model, "prompt": prompt, "stream": False}. If self.use_grammar: add "format": StatePatch.model_json_schema() to body — this is grammar-constrained output, Ollama constrains sampling to only produce tokens matching the StatePatch schema. Call response.raise_for_status(). Return response.json()["response"].

Write standalone def check_connection(base_url: str) -> bool. Import requests (sync). Try requests.get(f"{base_url}/api/tags", timeout=5). Return True on success, False on any exception.

Write async def main() that calls check_connection first. If False: print clear error message listing common causes — Ollama not running, wrong base_url, ngrok tunnel expired. If True: run sample agent call.

docs.title: examples/models/groq_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment: "Groq runs open models (Llama3, Mistral) with very low latency. Free tier. OpenAI-compatible API."

Write GroqAgent(LLMAgent). _call_model identical to OpenAIAgent._call_model but hardcodes base_url = "https://api.groq.com/openai/v1" and reads api_key from GROQ_API_KEY. Available models: llama3-8b-8192, llama3-70b-8192, mixtral-8x7b-32768.

docs.title: examples/models/rule_based_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment: "Not all agents need LLMs. Rule-based agents are deterministic, free, and instant."

Three standalone async functions:

async def priority_classifier(context: dict) -> StatePatch — reads customer_message from context.get("facts", {}). Checks for urgency keywords. Returns patch to "tasks.classification" with priority and matched keywords.

async def task_router(context: dict) -> StatePatch — reads classification. Maps priority to team. Returns patch to "tasks.routing" with assigned team.

async def completion_checker(context: dict) -> StatePatch — reads all tasks. If all done: returns patch setting "status" to "complete". Else: returns patch setting "facts.pending_count" to count of non-done tasks.

docs.title: examples/benchmark/run_benchmark.py
docs.text:
Write BenchmarkResult dataclass: model, use_grammar, attempt, success, retries_used, latency_seconds, error | None.

Write async def run_single_attempt(model, base_url, context, use_grammar, max_retries=3) -> BenchmarkResult. Records start time. Creates OllamaAgent. Tries to call it. On MaxRetriesExceeded: records failure. Records elapsed time and returns.

Write async def benchmark_model(model, base_url, n_runs=20) -> dict. Runs n_runs with grammar and n_runs without. Computes success rates, avg retries, avg latency for both conditions.

Module-level BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:

BENCHMARK_CONTEXT = {
    "goal": "Analyze architectural differences between monolithic and microservices systems",
    "tasks": {"task_1": {"description": "identify three core differences", "status": "pending"}},
    "facts": {"domain": "software architecture", "depth": "technical"}
}

async def main() — loads dotenv, gets base_url and model list from env, checks connection, benchmarks each model, prints formatted table, saves JSON results.

docs.title: docs/guides/llm-integration.md
docs.text:
Written entirely for users — no mention of Colab.

Title: "Connecting LLMs to agentstatelib"

Opening: agentstatelib never calls a model. Your agent functions do.

Section 1 — The minimal pattern. Five lines, any provider.

Section 2 — The retry-with-correction pattern. Why it matters. The LLMAgent base class. The one-method subclass pattern.

Section 3 — Provider examples. One paragraph per provider, link to example file. OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, Groq, rule-based.

Section 4 — Structured output reliability. Space for benchmark numbers. Grammar-constrained output explanation for Ollama.

Section 5 — Mixed-model workflows. GPT-4o for planner, Llama3-8B for researcher, rule-based for classifier.

Section 6 — Production. Token counting, Redis caching, semaphore rate limiting, cost tracking as facts.

docs.title: docs/guides/local-models.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — Why local models. Privacy, cost, control.

Section 2 — Setting up Ollama. Point to ollama.com. Basic commands.

Section 3 — Grammar-constrained output. Explain the format parameter. Benchmark numbers go here.

Section 4 — Retry loop. Even with grammar constraints, use the retry loop.

Section 5 — Choosing a model. Table of models with VRAM requirements and observed reliability.

Section 6 — Remote Ollama. base_url is the only thing that changes. Docker one-liner. Any server IP.

docs.title: Phase 4 · Week 7 · OpenTelemetry · Jaeger · WorkflowSummary · v0.4
Context

docs.title: agentstatelib/observability/tracing.py
docs.text:
Module docstring: "OpenTelemetry instrumentation for agentstatelib. Optional — install with pip install agentstate-lib[otel]. When not configured a no-op tracer runs silently with zero performance impact."

All OTel imports inside try: ... except ImportError:. Set HAS_OTEL = True on success, HAS_OTEL = False on failure.

Write _NoOpSpan class. Implements: __enter__(self) -> "_NoOpSpan" returns self. __exit__(self, *args) -> None does nothing. set_attribute(self, key: str, value: Any) -> None does nothing. record_exception(self, exc: Exception) -> None does nothing.

Write _NoOpTracer class. start_as_current_span(self, name: str, **kwargs) -> _NoOpSpan returns _NoOpSpan().

Write module-level _tracer: Any = None.

Write setup_tracing(service_name: str = "agentstatelib", exporter_endpoint: str = "http://localhost:4317") -> None. If HAS_OTEL is False: raise ImportError("OTel packages required. Install with: pip install agentstate-lib[otel]"). Create TracerProvider(). Create OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint=exporter_endpoint, insecure=True). Create BatchSpanProcessor(exporter). Call provider.add_span_processor(processor). Call trace.set_tracer_provider(provider). Set _tracer = trace.get_tracer(service_name). Docstring: "Configure OpenTelemetry tracing. Call once before any graph.run() calls. Traces are exported to the configured endpoint — Jaeger locally, Datadog or Honeycomb in production."

Write get_tracer() -> Any. If _tracer is None: return _NoOpTracer(). Return _tracer. Docstring: "Get the configured tracer, or a no-op tracer if setup_tracing() has not been called. All instrumented code uses this so tracing is always optional."

docs.title: agentstatelib/observability/analysis.py
docs.text:
Import Literal, Any from typing. Import BaseModel, Field from pydantic. Import all event types from agentstatelib.core.events.

Write AnomalyFlag model. Fields: rule_name (str), description (str), severity (Literal["warning", "error"]). Docstring: "A flag raised by an anomaly detection rule. warning severity is informational. error severity indicates a workflow that should be investigated."

Write AgentStats model. Fields: agent_id (str), patch_count (int, default 0), error_count (int, default 0). Docstring: "Aggregated statistics for one agent across a workflow run."

Write WorkflowSummary model. Fields: workflow_id (str), total_duration_seconds (float), total_patches (int), total_conflicts (int), conflict_rate (float — conflicts divided by max(total_patches, 1) to avoid division by zero), agent_stats (dict[str, AgentStats], default_factory=dict), is_anomalous (bool, default False), anomaly_flags (list[AnomalyFlag], default_factory=list). Docstring: "Statistics for a completed workflow run derived from the event log. Use analyze_workflow() to compute."

Write analyze_workflow(events: list[StateEvent]) -> WorkflowSummary. Iterate all events once. Track: start_time from the first WorkflowStarted event timestamp, end_time from the first WorkflowCompleted event timestamp (default to last event timestamp if no WorkflowCompleted), per-agent patch counts from PatchApplied events (key by agent_id), per-agent error counts from AgentErrored events, total conflict count from ConflictDetected events. Compute total_duration_seconds = end_time - start_time. Compute conflict_rate. Build agent_stats dict. Apply anomaly rules: if conflict_rate > 0.2 append AnomalyFlag(rule_name="high_conflict_rate", description=f"Conflict rate {conflict_rate:.1%} exceeds 20%", severity="warning"). If total_duration_seconds > 300 append flag. If any agent has patch_count == 0 append flag for that agent with description "Agent produced no patches — possible dead agent". Set is_anomalous = any(f.severity == "error" for f in flags). Return WorkflowSummary.

docs.title: agentstatelib/router/graph.py (add OTel instrumentation)
docs.text:
Add at the top of the file (after existing imports): from agentstatelib.observability.tracing import get_tracer.

In run(): add with get_tracer().start_as_current_span("workflow.run") as span: wrapping the entire method body. Inside: span.set_attribute("workflow.id", state.workflow_id). span.set_attribute("workflow.type", state.workflow_type). span.set_attribute("workflow.start_agents", str(start_ids)).

In _execute_round(): add with get_tracer().start_as_current_span("round") as span: wrapping the method body. Inside: span.set_attribute("round.agent_count", len(agent_ids)). span.set_attribute("round.agents", str(sorted(agent_ids))). After conflict resolution: if result.conflicts: span.set_attribute("round.conflict_count", len(result.conflicts)).

In _call_one() inside _run_agents_parallel(): add with get_tracer().start_as_current_span(f"agent.{agent_id}") as span: wrapping the body. span.set_attribute("agent.id", agent_id). After the call succeeds: span.set_attribute("agent.patch_target", patch.target). span.set_attribute("agent.patch_reason", patch.reason). span.set_attribute("agent.success", True). In the except block: span.record_exception(e). span.set_attribute("agent.success", False). Then re-raise.

docs.title: agentstatelib/observability/__init__.py
docs.text: Export setup_tracing, get_tracer from agentstatelib.observability.tracing. Export WorkflowSummary, analyze_workflow, AnomalyFlag, AgentStats from agentstatelib.observability.analysis. Set __all__.

docs.title: tests/unit/test_tracing.py
docs.text:
Write three tests:

test_get_tracer_without_setup_returns_noop — call get_tracer() without calling setup_tracing(). Assert the result has start_as_current_span method. Use it as a context manager. Assert no exception.

test_noop_span_all_methods_do_nothing — get no-op tracer. In with tracer.start_as_current_span("test") as span:: call span.set_attribute("k", "v"), span.record_exception(ValueError("test")). Assert no exception raised.

test_graph_runs_without_tracing_configured — create a simple one-agent graph. Run it without calling setup_tracing(). Assert it completes and returns correct state. Instrumentation must be completely invisible.

docs.title: docs/concepts/observability.md
docs.text:
Opening: "Most multi-agent frameworks are black boxes. You know what went in and what came out. agentstatelib is the opposite — every agent call, state change, conflict, and routing decision is recorded."

Section 1 — Three signals. Event log (what happened, always on), OTel traces (how long each part took, optional), WorkflowSummary (aggregate statistics, computed from event log).

Section 2 — The analogy. Datadog traces HTTP requests across microservices to find which service is slow or failing. agentstatelib traces agent calls across rounds to find which agent is slow, which produces conflicts, which is failing. Same mental model applied to AI workflows.

Section 3 — Why no existing library does this. LangSmith is LangChain-only. CrewAI and AutoGen have no observability layer. agentstatelib observability is framework-agnostic because it instruments the coordination layer, not the model calls.

docs.title: docs/guides/opentelemetry.md
docs.text:
Step 1 — Install and start Jaeger. Exact docker command. Verify at localhost:16686.

Step 2 — Install OTel extra. pip install agentstate-lib[otel].

Step 3 — Configure. Call setup_tracing(service_name="my-app") once before any graph.run(). No other code changes needed.

Step 4 — Run a workflow. Spans emit automatically.

Step 5 — Read the trace. Open Jaeger. Select service. Find trace. Read the waterfall. Explain: parent span is the workflow, child spans are rounds, grandchild spans are individual agent calls.

Step 6 — What to look for. Which agent span has the longest duration (slowest model call). Which rounds have conflict_count > 0. Which agent spans have success=False.

Production backends. Datadog: change exporter endpoint to https://trace.agent.datadoghq.com and add API key header. Honeycomb: endpoint is https://api.honeycomb.io:443/ with API key header.

WorkflowSummary section. Show analyze_workflow(events) call. Walk through each field. Show how to check summary.is_anomalous and read summary.anomaly_flags.

import asyncio
from agentstatelib.observability.tracing import setup_tracing
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch

setup_tracing(service_name='agentstatelib-dev')

async def main():
    graph = AgentGraph()

    @graph.node('planner')
    async def planner(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='planner', target='facts.plan',
                         value='done', reason='planned')

    @graph.node('researcher')

                         value=['s1', 's2'], reason='found sources')

    graph.edge('planner', 'researcher', lambda s: 'plan' in s.get('facts', {}))
    state = SharedState(goal='OTel test')
    await graph.run(state, start='planner')
    print('Workflow done. Check Jaeger at http://localhost:16686')
    print('Select service: agentstatelib-dev')

asyncio.run(main())
"

docs.title: Phase 4 · Week 8 · Terminal Dashboard · Web Dashboard · v0.4
Context
docs.text: Two deliverables this week. The terminal dashboard (rich.Live) for developers who want live output in their terminal. The self-hosted web dashboard (single HTML file served by FastAPI) for non-technical users who want to open a browser and see everything — workflow list, event timeline, live view, and replay scrubber. This is your LangSmith equivalent for self-hosted use.

docs.title: Files to work in
docs.text:
1. agentstatelib/observability/dashboard.py (terminal dashboard)
  2. agentstatelib/api/dashboard.py (web dashboard HTML)
  3. agentstatelib/api/routes.py (replace placeholder route)
  4. agentstatelib/observability/__init__.py (update exports)
  5. tests/unit/test_dashboard.py
  6. docs/guides/dashboard.md
  7. mkdocs.yml (add dashboard guide)

docs.title: agentstatelib/observability/dashboard.py
docs.text:
Module docstring: "Live terminal dashboard for agentstatelib workflows. Install with pip install agentstate-lib[dashboard]. Pass an asyncio.Queue as event_queue to AgentGraph.run() and the same queue to WorkflowDashboard. Run both concurrently with asyncio.gather."

All rich imports inside try: ... except ImportError:. Set HAS_RICH = True/False.

Write WorkflowDashboard class.

__init__(self, event_queue: asyncio.Queue) — stores the queue. Initializes: self._events: list = [], self._start_time: float = time.time(), self._current_agent: str = "starting...", self._conflict_count: int = 0, self._patch_count: int = 0.

_build_display(self) -> Any — returns a rich renderable. Create a Table with four columns: Time (width 8), Agent (width 16), Event (width 20), Target (width 30). Set table border style to minimal. For each of the last 15 events in self._events: format the row. Color-code: PatchApplied rows use green style, ConflictDetected rows use yellow, WorkflowStarted and WorkflowCompleted rows use blue, AgentErrored rows use red. The Time column shows f"{event.timestamp - self._start_time:.1f}s". The Target column shows the target attribute for PatchApplied events, the path attribute for ConflictDetected events, and empty string for others. Wrap the table in a Panel with title showing current agent and subtitle showing f"{self._patch_count} patches · {self._conflict_count} conflicts · {time.time() - self._start_time:.1f}s elapsed".

async def run(self) -> None — if HAS_RICH is False: raise ImportError("rich required. Install with: pip install agentstate-lib[dashboard]"). Create live = rich.live.Live(refresh_per_second=4). Enter with live:. In a loop: try event = await asyncio.wait_for(self._event_queue.get(), timeout=0.25). If event is None: break (sentinel received). Append to self._events. If isinstance(event, PatchApplied): self._patch_count += 1, update self._current_agent = event.agent_id. If isinstance(event, ConflictDetected): self._conflict_count += 1. Call live.update(self._build_display()). On asyncio.TimeoutError: call live.update(self._build_display()) to keep elapsed time ticking. Continue.

stop(self) -> None — puts None sentinel into queue. Docstring: "Signal the dashboard to stop after current event is processed."

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/dashboard.py
docs.text:
This file contains the complete self-hosted web dashboard as a Python string constant. The HTML is served by FastAPI at /dashboard.

Write DASHBOARD_HTML: str as a module-level constant. This is a complete HTML document in a triple-quoted string. The document must be fully self-contained — no external CDN dependencies, no npm, no build step. Everything is inline CSS and inline JavaScript.

The HTML structure:

<head> — title "agentstatelib dashboard", a <style> block with CSS. The CSS defines: --bg: #0f1117, --surface: #1a1d27, --surface2: #252836, --border: #2d3142, --text: #e2e8f0, --text-muted: #718096, --green: #48bb78, --yellow: #ecc94b, --blue: #63b3ed, --red: #fc8181, --purple: #b794f4. Layout: full viewport height flexbox with a 260px sidebar on the left and main content on the right. Sidebar has a header with the agentstatelib logo text and a list of workflow items. Main area has a tab bar (Detail, Live, Replay) and content panels.

<body> structure: - <div id="app"> containing: - <div class="sidebar"> with <div class="logo">agentstatelib</div> and <div id="workflow-list"> (populated by JS) - <div class="main"> with <div class="tabs"> and <div id="content"> <script> block at the bottom with all JavaScript:

Configuration constants:

const API_KEY = new URLSearchParams(window.location.search).get('key') || 'dev-key-123';
const BASE_URL = window.location.origin;

State variables: let currentWorkflowId = null, let eventSource = null, let liveEvents = [], let allEvents = [], let replayIndex = 0.

Helper apiFetch(path, options = {}). Adds x-api-key header to every request. Returns response.json(). Handles 401 by showing an alert that tells the user to add ?key=YOUR_KEY to the URL.

loadWorkflowList(). Calls apiFetch("/v1/workflows"). Renders each workflow_id as a <div class="workflow-item"> in the sidebar. Clicking one calls selectWorkflow(id). Call this on page load and every 5 seconds via setInterval.

selectWorkflow(workflowId). Sets currentWorkflowId. Highlights the selected sidebar item. Calls loadDetail(workflowId).

loadDetail(workflowId). Calls apiFetch("/v1/workflows/${workflowId}") to get current state. Calls apiFetch("/v1/workflows/${workflowId}/events-list") — wait, you do not have an events list endpoint yet. Add a GET /v1/workflows/{id}/events-list endpoint to routes.py this week that returns all events as a JSON array (not SSE). Store them in allEvents. Renders the Detail tab.

renderDetailTab(events, state). Builds an HTML string for the event timeline. Each event is a <div class="event-row"> with colored left border by type. Inside: timestamp, agent badge, event type label, and an expandable details section. PatchApplied rows show target, reason, and a collapsible diff showing old_value and new_value as formatted JSON. ConflictDetected rows show path, winner badge (green), loser badge (red), and strategy label. Clicking a row toggles the details section open/closed.

startLiveView(workflowId). Closes any existing EventSource. Creates eventSource = new EventSource(${BASE_URL}/v1/workflows/${workflowId}/events?key=${API_KEY}). SSE does not support custom headers so pass the key as a query parameter — update the streaming route in routes.py to also accept key as a query parameter in addition to the header. On eventSource.onmessage: parse JSON, append to liveEvents, call renderLiveRow(event) to append a row to the live table. Update the stats line. The live table structure matches the terminal dashboard: Time, Agent, Event, Target columns with the same color coding.

renderReplayTab(events). Creates an HTML slider <input type="range" min="0" max="${events.length}" value="0">. Below it a <pre> block for the reconstructed state JSON. On slider input: call replayToIndex(parseInt(slider.value)).

replayToIndex(index). Reimplements the replay logic in JavaScript. Start with let state = {}. For each PatchApplied event in events.slice(0, index): call setNested(state, event.target, event.new_value). Update the pre block with JSON.stringify(state, null, 2). Also updates a progress label showing "Step ${index} of ${events.length}".

setNested(obj, path, value). JavaScript implementation of set_nested. Splits on .. Creates intermediate objects as needed. Sets the final key.

formatTimestamp(ts). Returns a relative time string like "2.3s" based on the difference from the workflow start time.

Event type colors. A lookup object mapping event type strings to CSS color variables.

Initialization. On DOMContentLoaded: call loadWorkflowList(). Set up tab click handlers. Set up the 5-second refresh interval for the workflow list.

Add to agentstatelib/api/routes.py:

GET /workflows/{workflow_id}/events-list — auth and store. Returns all events as a JSON array: {"events": [event.model_dump() for event in events], "count": len(events)}. This is the non-streaming endpoint the web dashboard uses to load history.

Update the streaming endpoint to also accept the API key as a query parameter key: str | None = None in addition to the header, and check both. This is needed because EventSource in browsers cannot set custom headers.

Replace the dashboard placeholder route in app.py: import DASHBOARD_HTML from agentstatelib.api.dashboard. Change the placeholder to return HTMLResponse(content=DASHBOARD_HTML).

docs.text:
Write three tests:

docs.title: docs/guides/dashboard.md
docs.text:
Title: "Workflow dashboards"

Section 1 — Terminal dashboard (for developers). What users see. The asyncio.gather pattern. Note: use a real terminal (Windows Terminal, iTerm2, Linux terminal), not an IDE integrated terminal — rich.Live renders best in a proper terminal emulator. Tell users to screenshot and add to their README.

Section 2 — Web dashboard (for teams). What users see when they open http://localhost:8000/dashboard. Walk through the three tabs: Detail (event timeline with expandable rows), Live (real-time event stream), Replay (slider-based state reconstruction). Explain the API key in the URL: append ?key=YOUR_KEY to the dashboard URL. Show the full URL pattern: http://localhost:8000/dashboard?key=dev-key-123.

Section 3 — What the web dashboard replaces. For developers: running Jaeger locally for traces. For product managers and researchers: asking engineers to explain what their agents did. The web dashboard is readable by anyone — the event timeline and replay view require no technical knowledge to interpret.

Section 4 — Limitations vs the hosted future. The self-hosted dashboard runs alongside your server — it has access to one server's data. The future hosted platform (post-v1.0) stores data from multiple deployments and provides team sharing, search across workflow runs, cost analytics, and evaluation tooling similar to LangSmith.

docs.title: Test and demo commands for Week 8
docs.text:
pytest tests/ -v
mypy agentstatelib/ --strict

# Terminal dashboard demo — run in Windows Terminal, not VS Code terminal
python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.memory.store import InMemoryStore
from agentstatelib.observability.dashboard import WorkflowDashboard

async def main():
    queue = asyncio.Queue()
    store = InMemoryStore()
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)

    @graph.node('planner')
    async def planner(ctx):
        await asyncio.sleep(0.8)
        return StatePatch(agent_id='planner', target='facts.plan',
                         value='done', reason='planned')

    @graph.node('analyst_a')
    async def analyst_a(ctx):
        await asyncio.sleep(1.2)
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_a', target='artifacts.result_a',
                         value='analysis a', reason='done')

    @graph.node('analyst_b')
    async def analyst_b(ctx):
        await asyncio.sleep(1.0)
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_b', target='artifacts.result_b',
                         value='analysis b', reason='done')

    @graph.node('writer')
    async def writer(ctx):
        await asyncio.sleep(0.9)
        return StatePatch(agent_id='writer', target='artifacts.draft',
                         value='Final draft', reason='wrote')

    graph.edge('planner', 'analyst_a')
    graph.edge('planner', 'analyst_b')
    graph.edge('analyst_a', 'writer')
    graph.edge('analyst_b', 'writer')

    dashboard = WorkflowDashboard(queue)
    state = SharedState(goal='Dashboard demo')
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        graph.run(state, start='planner', event_queue=queue),
        dashboard.run(),
    )
    print('Done:', list(results[0].artifacts.keys()))

asyncio.run(main())
"

# Web dashboard demo
$env:AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS = "dev-key-123"
uvicorn agentstatelib.api.app:app --reload

async def main():

        return StatePatch(agent_id='researcher', target='artifacts.result', value='found', reason='found')
    graph.edge('planner', 'researcher', lambda s: 'plan' in s.get('facts', {}))
    state = SharedState(goal='Web dashboard test workflow')
    await graph.run(state, start='planner')
    print('Workflow complete. Open http://localhost:8000/dashboard?key=dev-key-123')

asyncio.run(main())
"
