docs.title: Phase 5 · Week 9 · Tests · Property Tests · Docker · v0.5
Context
docs.text: Property-based tests use hypothesis to find edge cases you did not think of. They are especially valuable for a coordination library where an unexpected input to set_nested or replay could produce a subtle bug. Docker means users can run the full stack — API, Redis, Jaeger — with one command.

docs.title: Files to work in
docs.text:
1. tests/conftest.py (fill in shared fixtures)
  2. tests/property/test_patch_properties.py
  3. tests/property/test_replay_properties.py
  4. tests/property/test_invariant_properties.py
  5. Dockerfile
  6. docker-compose.yml
  7. docs/guides/testing.md
uv add --dev hypothesis

docs.title: tests/conftest.py
docs.text:
Replace the placeholder comment with these shared fixtures that all test files can import via the conftest mechanism.

sample_state — returns SharedState(goal="test goal", workflow_id="fixed-test-id-00000001"). The fixed workflow_id makes event log assertions deterministic.

in_memory_store — returns InMemoryStore().

sqlite_store(tmp_path) — returns SQLiteStore(str(tmp_path / "test.db")).

sample_patch — returns StatePatch(agent_id="test_agent", target="facts.test", value="test_value", reason="test reason").

stub_planner — async function (not a fixture, a plain async def at module level) that accepts ctx: dict and returns StatePatch(agent_id="planner", target="tasks.research", value={"description": "research task", "status": "pending"}, reason="planner created task").

stub_researcher — returns StatePatch(agent_id="researcher", target="artifacts.sources", value=["source1", "source2"], reason="researcher found sources").

slow_stub(agent_id: str, target: str, value: Any) — factory function that returns an async function which awaits asyncio.sleep(0.05) then returns a patch. Used for timing-sensitive tests.

docs.title: tests/property/test_patch_properties.py
docs.text:
Import given, strategies as st from hypothesis. Import pytest. Import set_nested, get_nested, apply_patch, StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state.

Write four property-based tests:

test_set_nested_roundtrip — strategy: @given(st.lists(st.text(alphabet=st.characters(whitelist_categories=("Ll", "Lu")), min_size=1, max_size=10), min_size=1, max_size=5)). Join the list with "." to make a path. Call get_nested(set_nested({}, path, 42), path). Assert the result equals 42 for any valid dotted path with no empty components.

test_apply_patch_never_mutates_original — strategy: @given(st.text(min_size=1), st.text(min_size=1, max_size=20), st.integers()). Create state = SharedState(goal=goal_text). Record original_dump = state.model_dump(). Create patch = StatePatch(agent_id="a", target=target_text, value=int_value, reason="test"). Call apply_patch(state, patch). Assert state.model_dump() == original_dump. The original must never change.

test_apply_patch_always_returns_shared_state — same strategy. Assert isinstance(apply_patch(state, patch), SharedState) is always True.

test_resolve_batch_winners_subset_of_paths — strategy: @given(st.lists(st.text(min_size=1, max_size=10), min_size=1, max_size=10)). Create one patch per path string. Call resolve_batch. Assert len(result.winners) <= len(set(paths)). The number of winners can never exceed the number of unique paths.

docs.title: tests/property/test_replay_properties.py
docs.text:
Import given, strategies as st from hypothesis. Import needed modules.

test_replay_is_deterministic — strategy: @given(st.integers(min_value=1, max_value=10)). Create n PatchApplied events each setting a different facts.key_{i} to i. Add one WorkflowStarted event at the beginning. Call replay(events) twice with the same list. Assert state1.model_dump() == state2.model_dump(). Replay must be deterministic — same input always produces same output.

test_replay_respects_event_order — strategy: @given(st.integers(min_value=2, max_value=8)). Create n events each setting facts.counter to an increasing integer. Assert that replay(events).facts["counter"] equals the value from the last event, not any intermediate value.

docs.title: tests/property/test_invariant_properties.py
docs.text:
test_empty_state_never_violates_builtin_checkers — strategy: @given(st.text(min_size=1, max_size=100)). Create SharedState(goal=text) with no tasks, goals, or artifacts. Call check_all(state, [TasksReferenceExistingGoals(), CompletedGoalsHaveNoBlockingTasks()]). Assert result is an empty list. An empty workflow state must never produce violations.

test_valid_task_with_valid_goal_no_violation — create a state, add a real Goal to state.goals, apply a patch adding a Task with goal_id pointing to that goal. Assert no violations from TasksReferenceExistingGoals().

docs.title: Dockerfile
docs.text:
Two-stage build.

First stage named builder. FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder. WORKDIR /app. Copy pyproject.toml and README.md first (before source) so Docker can cache the dependency layer separately from source changes. Run pip install uv --no-cache-dir. Run uv pip install --system ".[api,otel,dashboard]" --no-cache. Then COPY agentstatelib/ ./agentstatelib/.

Second stage named runtime. FROM python:3.12-slim AS runtime. WORKDIR /app. COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages /usr/local/lib/python3.12/site-packages. COPY --from=builder /usr/local/bin /usr/local/bin. COPY --from=builder /app/agentstatelib ./agentstatelib. EXPOSE 8000. ENV AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS=dev-key-123. ENV AGENTSTATE_DB_PATH=/data/agentstatelib.db. Add VOLUME ["/data"] so the SQLite file persists when the container is stopped. CMD ["uvicorn", "agentstatelib.api.app:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"].

docs.title: docker-compose.yml
docs.text:
Write a compose file with three services:

api service: build: ., ports: ["8000:8000"], environment: AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS=${AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS:-dev-key-123} (reads from host env or defaults), AGENTSTATE_DB_PATH=/data/agentstatelib.db, OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://jaeger:4317, volumes: [agentstatelib_data:/data], depends_on: [redis, jaeger].

redis service: image: redis:7-alpine, ports: ["6379:6379"], volumes: [redis_data:/data].

jaeger service: image: jaegertracing/all-in-one:latest, ports: ["16686:16686", "4317:4317"], environment: COLLECTOR_OTLP_ENABLED=true.

At the bottom: volumes: agentstatelib_data:, redis_data:. Named volumes survive docker-compose down and persist data.

docs.title: docs/guides/testing.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — Testing your own workflows. Use InMemoryStore in all tests — no filesystem setup, no cleanup, works identically to SQLiteStore. Show a conftest.py pattern with a graph fixture that creates a standard AgentGraph with stub agents registered.

Section 2 — Stub agent functions. Show the pattern: a plain async function that returns a hardcoded StatePatch. Explain that this tests coordination logic (routing, conflicts, invariants) without any model calls. Show how to parametrize tests with different conflict resolver strategies.

Section 3 — Testing custom InvariantCheckers. Show how to call checker.check(state) directly in a unit test. You do not need to run a full graph to test invariant logic.

Section 4 — Property-based testing with hypothesis. Explain what property tests catch that example-based tests miss. Show the basic @given pattern with strategies. Recommend: always test that apply_patch does not mutate the original state, always test that replay is deterministic.

docs.title: Test and publish commands for Week 9
docs.text:
pytest tests/ -v

pytest tests/property/ -v --hypothesis-seed=0

# Run with more hypothesis examples to find edge cases
pytest tests/property/ --hypothesis-settings="max_examples=500" -v

docker build -t agentstate-lib:dev .

docker run -p 8000:8000 agentstate-lib:dev
curl http://localhost:8000/health

docker-compose up -d
docker-compose ps
curl -H "x-api-key: dev-key-123" http://localhost:8000/health
docker-compose down

docs.title: Done when
docs.text: All unit and integration tests pass. Property tests pass with --hypothesis-seed=0. Docker image builds and health endpoint works inside the container. docker-compose up starts all three services cleanly.

docs.title: Phase 5 · Week 10 · API Hardening · Redis Cache · Rate Limiting · v0.5
Context
docs.text: Redis adds two production-critical features: response caching (same context to same agent returns the cached StatePatch instead of calling the model again — cuts costs and latency) and rate limiting (prevents a single API key from overwhelming the server).

docs.title: Files to work in
docs.text:
1. agentstatelib/api/cache.py
  2. agentstatelib/api/auth.py (add RateLimiter)
  3. agentstatelib/api/app.py (wire cache and rate limiter via app.state)
  4. agentstatelib/api/routes.py (update auth dependency to check rate limit)
  5. tests/unit/test_cache.py
  6. docs/guides/production.md
  7. mkdocs.yml (add production guide)
uv add redis
docker run -d --name redis -p 6379:6379 redis:7-alpine

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/cache.py
docs.text:
Wrap all redis imports in try: import redis.asyncio as aioredis; HAS_REDIS = True then except ImportError: aioredis = None; HAS_REDIS = False.

Write StatePatchCache class. Docstring: "Optional Redis-backed cache for StatePatch responses. When a LLM-backed agent receives the same context twice, the cached StatePatch is returned instead of calling the model again. This cuts LLM API costs in workflows where the same context is seen repeatedly."

__init__(self, redis_url: str = "redis://localhost:6379", default_ttl: int = 3600) — if HAS_REDIS is False: raise ImportError("redis package required for StatePatchCache. Install with: pip install redis"). Store self.default_ttl = default_ttl. Create self._redis = aioredis.from_url(redis_url, encoding="utf-8", decode_responses=True).

def _cache_key(self, agent_id: str, context: dict) -> str — imports hashlib and json (inside the method is fine since these are stdlib). Build a stable string: f"{agent_id}:{json.dumps(context, sort_keys=True, default=str)}". Call hashlib.sha256(stable_string.encode()).hexdigest(). Prefix with "agentstate:patch:" and return. The sort_keys=True ensures the same dict always produces the same JSON string regardless of insertion order. The default=str handles non-JSON-serializable values gracefully.

async def get(self, agent_id: str, context: dict) -> StatePatch | None — call raw = await self._redis.get(self._cache_key(agent_id, context)). If raw is None: return None. Return StatePatch.model_validate_json(raw).

async def set(self, agent_id: str, context: dict, patch: StatePatch, ttl: int | None = None) -> None — call await self._redis.setex(self._cache_key(agent_id, context), ttl or self.default_ttl, patch.model_dump_json()).

async def delete(self, agent_id: str, context: dict) -> None — call await self._redis.delete(self._cache_key(agent_id, context)).

async def close(self) -> None — call await self._redis.aclose().

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/auth.py (add RateLimiter)
docs.text:
Add the following after the existing verify_api_key function.

Write RateLimiter class. Docstring: "Redis-backed rate limiter using a sliding window counter. Each API key gets a separate counter that resets after window_seconds. Returns False when the limit is exceeded."

__init__(self, redis_url: str = "redis://localhost:6379", max_requests: int = 100, window_seconds: int = 60) — if HAS_REDIS is False: raise ImportError. Store both limits. Create self._redis = aioredis.from_url(redis_url, encoding="utf-8", decode_responses=True). Import aioredis inside a try/except at the top of the file alongside the existing redis import.

async def check(self, api_key: str) -> bool — build key f"agentstate:ratelimit:{api_key}". Call count = await self._redis.incr(key). If count == 1: call await self._redis.expire(key, self.window_seconds) — set expiry only on the first increment to establish the window. If count > self.max_requests: return False. Return True.

async def close(self) -> None — close the redis connection.

Update verify_api_key to accept an optional rate_limiter: RateLimiter | None = None parameter. When a rate_limiter is provided and await rate_limiter.check(api_key) returns False: raise HTTPException(status_code=429, detail={"error_code": "rate_limit_exceeded", "message": f"Rate limit exceeded. Maximum {rate_limiter.max_requests} requests per {rate_limiter.window_seconds} seconds."}).

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/app.py (wire cache and rate limiter)
docs.text:
Update create_app to accept two new optional parameters: redis_url: str | None = None (defaults to None meaning no Redis) and include rate_limit_max: int = 100, rate_limit_window: int = 60.

Inside create_app: if redis_url is not None, try to create a StatePatchCache(redis_url) and a RateLimiter(redis_url, rate_limit_max, rate_limit_window), catch ImportError and set both to None with a warning print. Store both on app.state.cache and app.state.rate_limiter. If redis_url is None, set both to None.

Add a shutdown event handler using @app.on_event("shutdown") (or lifespan pattern) that calls await app.state.cache.close() and await app.state.rate_limiter.close() if they are not None. This cleans up Redis connections properly.

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/routes.py (update rate limit check)
docs.text: Update get_store dependency to also return the rate_limiter: rename it to get_app_state or keep get_store and add a separate get_rate_limiter(request: Request) -> RateLimiter | None dependency. Inject rate_limiter = Depends(get_rate_limiter) alongside verify_api_key = Depends(verify_api_key) on all protected routes. Pass rate_limiter to verify_api_key calls — but since FastAPI dependencies do not compose this way easily, the cleanest approach is: add rate_limiter: RateLimiter | None = Depends(get_rate_limiter) as a parameter on each protected endpoint function, and call await verify_rate_limit(api_key, rate_limiter) as the first line of each function where verify_rate_limit is a helper that checks and raises 429 if needed.

docs.title: tests/unit/test_cache.py
docs.text:
Write four tests. These require a running Redis instance — mark them with @pytest.mark.skipif(not HAS_REDIS, reason="Redis not available").

test_cache_miss_returns_none — create a cache. Call get. Assert None is returned for a key that was never set.

test_cache_set_and_get — create a cache. Create a StatePatch. Call set. Call get with the same agent_id and context. Assert the returned patch equals the original (compare model_dump()).

test_cache_key_stable_for_same_context — create a cache. Compute _cache_key("agent", {"b": 2, "a": 1}). Compute again with {"a": 1, "b": 2}. Assert both keys are identical — dict order must not affect the key.

test_rate_limiter_blocks_after_limit — create RateLimiter with max_requests=3, window_seconds=60. Call check("test-key") four times. Assert the first three return True. Assert the fourth returns False.

docs.title: docs/guides/production.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — Environment variables. Full table: AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS (comma-separated valid keys, required), AGENTSTATE_DB_PATH (SQLite file path, default agentstatelib.db), REDIS_URL (Redis connection string, optional, enables caching and rate limiting), OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT (OTel endpoint, optional).

Section 2 — Docker deployment. Show the docker-compose up command. Show how to set AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS as an environment variable on the host. Explain the named volumes for persistence.

Section 3 — Rate limiting. Explain the sliding window model. Show how to configure limits. Explain the 429 response and how clients should handle it with exponential backoff.

Section 4 — Response caching. Explain when caching helps (workflows where the same agent receives the same context repeatedly). Show how the SHA-256 cache key works. Note TTL configuration.

Section 5 — Health monitoring. Show how to hook GET /health into a monitoring system. Explain what a 200 response means (server is up, store is accessible) and what to alert on.

Section 6 — Reverse proxy configuration. Show the Nginx location block needed for SSE streaming to work correctly — specifically the proxy_buffering off, proxy_cache off, and add_header X-Accel-Buffering no directives.

docs.title: Test and publish commands for Week 10
docs.text:
pytest tests/ -v

# Test with Redis running
docker run -d --name redis-test -p 6379:6379 redis:7-alpine
pytest tests/unit/test_cache.py -v
docker stop redis-test && docker rm redis-test

mypy agentstatelib/ --strict

python -m build
twine upload dist/*
git tag v0.5.0
git push origin v0.5.0
mike deploy --push --update-aliases 0.5 latest

docs.title: Done when
docs.text: All tests pass. Cache tests pass when Redis is running. Rate limiter blocks at the correct threshold. mypy is clean. v0.5.0 on PyPI.

docs.title: Phase 6 · Week 11 · API Audit · Type Stubs · ADRs · v1.0-alpha prep
Context
docs.text: Before shipping v1.0 you need three things: type stubs so IDEs give users full autocomplete, ADRs so the architectural decisions are documented and searchable, and a complete mypy --strict pass. This week is about library quality, not new features.

docs.title: Files to work in
docs.text:
1. agentstatelib/__init__.pyi
  2. agentstatelib/router/graph.pyi
  3. docs/decisions/ADR-001-event-sourcing.md
  4. docs/decisions/ADR-002-protocol-over-abc.md
  5. docs/decisions/ADR-003-patch-immutability.md
  6. docs/decisions/ADR-004-agentfn-callable.md
  7. docs/decisions/ADR-005-parallel-round-execution.md
  8. mkdocs.yml (add decisions section)

docs.title: API audit — do this before writing any files
docs.text: Go through every public name in agentstatelib/__all__. For each one: write a one-sentence description. If you cannot write it in one sentence, the name is doing too much or is poorly named — fix it before continuing. Check every public class has a class docstring. Check every public function has a function docstring with parameter descriptions. Run mypy agentstatelib/ --strict and fix every single error. Zero errors is the requirement to proceed to week 12.

docs.title: agentstatelib/__init__.pyi
docs.text:
Type stub files contain only signatures — no implementation. Every function body is .... Every class body lists method signatures and class attributes. These files tell mypy and IDEs the types of everything your package exports without executing any code.

Write type signatures for every name in __all__. For classes: write the class with its __init__ signature and every public method signature with full types. For __version__: write __version__: str. For __all__: write __all__: list[str]. For type aliases like AgentFn and EventQueue: write the alias using TypeAlias from typing.

Pay special attention to AgentGraph. Its node() method returns Callable[[AgentFn], AgentFn]. Its run() method is async def returning SharedState. Both of these need the exact correct return types in the stub.

docs.title: agentstatelib/router/graph.pyi
docs.text:
Write stubs specifically for AgentGraph. Focus on the public interface:

__init__ with all four optional parameters and their defaults. node(agent_id: str, context: list[str] | None = None) -> Callable[[AgentFn], AgentFn]. edge(from_agent: str, to_agent: str, condition: EdgeCondition | None = None) -> None. add_invariant(checker: InvariantChecker) -> None. async def run(state: SharedState, start: str | list[str], event_queue: EventQueue | None = None) -> SharedState.

Also export WorkflowEvent and EventQueue type aliases with their correct types.

docs.title: ADR files
docs.text:
Each ADR follows this exact structure:

# ADR-00N: Title

## Status
Accepted

## Context
[Two to three paragraphs. The problem you faced, options you considered,
what tradeoffs mattered. Be specific about what you actually tried or
evaluated, not hypothetical alternatives.]

## Decision
[One to two paragraphs. What you decided and the specific reason.]

## Consequences

### Positive
- [bullet list of what you gained]

### Negative
- [bullet list of what you gave up or what new complexity this introduces]

### Neutral
- [things that changed without being clearly good or bad]

ADR-001: Event sourcing over mutable state storage. Context: you needed to persist workflow state and wanted auditability. Options were (1) store only current state — simple but no history, (2) store full state snapshots — duplicates data, (3) store events only and derive state by replay — complex but complete history. You needed replay for the debugger and auditability for the competitive story against LangGraph. Decision: events only, state derived by replay, checkpoints for performance on long logs. Positive: full audit trail, any past state recoverable, replay debugger possible. Negative: replay is O(n) in event count (mitigated by checkpoints), event schema changes require migration. Neutral: state is never stored directly — only events.

ADR-002: Protocol over abstract base class for StateStore. Context: needed to define the persistence interface. Options were ABC (explicit, requires inheritance from agentstatelib), Protocol (structural typing, no inheritance). Decision: @runtime_checkable Protocol everywhere. Positive: any class with the right methods works without importing from agentstatelib, truly framework-agnostic, third-party databases work without adapters. Negative: less explicit — the Protocol requirement is not surfaced by IDEs as clearly as an ABC. Neutral: isinstance checks still work due to @runtime_checkable.

ADR-003: StatePatch immutability. Context: agents needed to update state. Options were direct mutation of SharedState, a mutable patch queue, or immutable StatePatch proposals. Decision: immutable StatePatch objects, apply_patch returns new SharedState. Positive: full attribution for every change, conflict detection possible because patches are objects not applied mutations, replay works because PatchApplied events store the patch details. Negative: creates new SharedState objects per patch (performance overhead for very large states), adds indirection for users who expect direct dict access. Neutral: agent code never imports SharedState — only returns StatePatch.

ADR-004: AgentFn as a callable type alias, not a class. Context: needed to define what an agent is. Options were require a base class, require a Protocol, or define a callable type alias. Decision: AgentFn = Callable[[dict], Awaitable[StatePatch]]. Positive: any async function works, no library imports required in agent code, works with any model provider, rule-based functions and LLM-backed functions are identical. Negative: less IDE discoverability than a class — users may not realize they need to return a StatePatch without reading docs. Neutral: LLMAgent in contrib provides an opt-in base class for users who want structure.

ADR-005: Round-based parallel execution over sequential or lock-based parallel. Context: needed an execution model for multiple agents. Options were (1) sequential one-at-a-time — simple, wastes parallelism, no conflicts possible, (2) parallel with shared mutable state — fast but requires locking, complex deadlock avoidance, (3) round-based batch — all agents in a round see same snapshot, patches collected and resolved as a batch. Decision: round-based. Positive: eliminates intra-round read-write races, natural fan-out topology, conflicts detectable as a batch operation, all agents in a round see guaranteed-consistent state. Negative: coordination overhead between rounds, users must understand the round model to reason about execution order. Neutral: within a round agents are truly concurrent, across rounds they are sequential.

docs.title: Update mkdocs.yml nav for Week 11
docs.text:
Add a top-level section:

  - Architecture:
    - ADR-001 Event Sourcing: decisions/ADR-001-event-sourcing.md
    - ADR-002 Protocol over ABC: decisions/ADR-002-protocol-over-abc.md
    - ADR-003 Patch Immutability: decisions/ADR-003-patch-immutability.md
    - ADR-004 AgentFn Callable: decisions/ADR-004-agentfn-callable.md
    - ADR-005 Parallel Rounds: decisions/ADR-005-parallel-round-execution.md

docs.title: Commands for Week 11

# API audit pass — fix every error before anything else
mypy agentstatelib/ --strict 2>&1 | head -50

# After fixing all errors
mypy agentstatelib/ --strict
# Must exit with code 0 and zero error lines

ruff check agentstatelib/

pytest tests/ -v

docs.title: Phase 6 · Week 12 · Package · CI/CD · Integrations · v1.0-alpha
Context
docs.text: CI/CD means every push runs tests automatically and every tag push publishes to PyPI and deploys docs without manual steps. The integration examples show your framework-agnostic claim is true. v1.0-alpha is the version you share publicly for feedback.

docs.title: Files to work in
docs.text:
1. pyproject.toml (finalize metadata)
  2. .github/workflows/test.yml
  3. .github/workflows/publish.yml
  4. .github/workflows/docs.yml
  5. examples/integrations/langchain_adapter.py
  6. examples/integrations/langgraph_adapter.py
  7. examples/integrations/litellm_agent.py
  8. CHANGELOG.md
  9. README.md (final version)
  10. docs/guides/integrations.md
  11. docs/guides/migration/v0.x-to-v1.md
  12. mkdocs.yml (add new sections)

docs.title: pyproject.toml (finalize)
docs.text:
Add [project.urls] section with Homepage, Documentation, Repository, Bug Tracker pointing to your GitHub URLs.

Add [project.keywords] with: ai, agents, multi-agent, coordination, observability, llm, openai, anthropic, ollama.

Add [project.classifiers] with Python version classifiers, Development Status 4 - Beta, License MIT, Topic Scientific/Engineering Artificial Intelligence.

Update version to "1.0.0a1" (Python alpha pre-release convention).

docs.title: .github/workflows/test.yml
docs.text:
Trigger on: push to main branch, and pull_request to main. Runs on: ubuntu-latest. Steps in order:

actions/checkout@v4 with no extra options.

actions/setup-python@v5 with python-version: "3.12".

pip install uv — single line shell command.

uv pip install --system ".[dev]" — installs all dev dependencies.

ruff check agentstatelib/ — lint check, fails CI if there are violations.

mypy agentstatelib/ --strict — type check, fails CI if there are errors.

pytest tests/ -v --tb=short — run tests with short traceback format for readable CI output.

Do not add any caching steps — keep the workflow simple. CI correctness matters more than CI speed at this stage.

docs.title: .github/workflows/publish.yml
docs.text:
Trigger on: push of tags matching v* (the tags: filter under on.push). Runs on ubuntu-latest. Needs id-token: write permission for trusted publishing (optional — can use PYPI_TOKEN secret instead).

Steps: checkout, Python setup, install uv, install build dependencies.

Critical step before publishing: mkdocs build --strict. If docs fail to build, abort the release — a version with broken docs must never reach PyPI. If this step fails the whole workflow fails.

Then: python -m build to create the dist/ artifacts. Then: twine upload dist/* using either PYPI_TOKEN from repository secrets or OIDC trusted publishing.

After successful upload: create a GitHub release using actions/create-release@v1 or softprops/action-gh-release@v2 with the tag name and auto-generated release notes.

docs.title: .github/workflows/docs.yml
docs.text:
Trigger on: push of tags matching v*. Runs on ubuntu-latest. Needs contents: write permission for mike to push to gh-pages.

Steps: actions/checkout@v4 with fetch-depth: 0 — mike requires full git history to manage versioned docs. Python setup. Install docs dependencies: uv pip install --system ".[docs]". Configure git user for the push: git config user.name "github-actions[bot]" and git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com". Extract version from tag: VERSION=$(echo ${{ github.ref_name }} | sed 's/^v//'). Run mike deploy --push --update-aliases $VERSION latest. Run mike set-default --push latest.

Note: add your GitHub repository's Pages settings to serve from the gh-pages branch. Go to Settings → Pages → Source → Deploy from branch → gh-pages → / (root).

docs.title: Add PYPI_TOKEN to GitHub secrets
docs.text: Go to PyPI → Account settings → API tokens → Add API token → scope to agentstate-lib project → copy the token. Go to GitHub → your repo → Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret → name PYPI_TOKEN → paste the token.

docs.title: examples/integrations/langchain_adapter.py
docs.text:
Comment block: "Shows how to wrap a LangChain chain as an agentstatelib AgentFn. The adapter is 10 lines — demonstrating that agentstatelib is indifferent to what produces the StatePatch."

Import StatePatch from agentstatelib. Import AgentFn from agentstatelib.router.types.

Write make_langchain_agent(chain: Any, agent_id: str, target: str, reason_prefix: str = "LangChain output") -> AgentFn. Inside: define async def agent(context: dict) -> StatePatch. The function calls result = await chain.ainvoke(context). If result is a string: return StatePatch(agent_id=agent_id, target=target, value=result, reason=f"{reason_prefix}: {result[:50]}"). If result is a dict with a "text" key: use that. Returns the inner agent function. The outer function is a factory that captures chain, agent_id, target, reason_prefix via closure.

Write a main() showing usage with a hypothetical LangChain chain. Comment explaining: register with graph.node("my_agent")(make_langchain_agent(my_chain, "my_agent", "artifacts.result")).

docs.title: examples/integrations/langgraph_adapter.py
docs.text:
Comment block: "Shows how agentstatelib SharedState can coexist with a LangGraph graph. Use agentstatelib for persistence and observability while using LangGraph for its specific node types if needed."

Show a pattern where a LangGraph graph is invoked inside an agentstatelib agent function. The agent receives context from agentstatelib, passes relevant parts to LangGraph, takes the result, and returns a StatePatch. The key insight: agentstatelib handles the outer loop (rounds, conflicts, event log) and LangGraph handles the inner loop (one agent's sub-graph). They compose without conflict.

docs.title: examples/integrations/litellm_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment block: "LiteLLM provides one interface for 100+ model providers. Use this when you want to switch between providers without changing agent code."

Import LLMAgent from agentstatelib.contrib.base_agent. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib.

Write LiteLLMAgent(LLMAgent). Additional __init__ param: litellm_model: str (e.g. "gpt-4o", "claude-3-opus-20240229", "ollama/llama3", "groq/llama3-8b-8192").

_call_model: import litellm inside the method. Call response = await litellm.acompletion(model=self.litellm_model, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]). Return response.choices[0].message.content.

Add a comment listing example model strings for different providers. Show that switching from OpenAI to Anthropic to Groq to Ollama requires only changing the litellm_model string.

docs.title: CHANGELOG.md
docs.text: Write a changelog following Keep a Changelog format. Add entries for v0.1.0, v0.2.0, v0.2.1, v0.3.0, v0.3.1, v0.4.0, v0.5.0, and v1.0.0-alpha. Each entry has: date, and sections Added, Changed, Fixed. Keep entries concise — one line per item.

docs.title: README.md (final version)
docs.text:
Header: name, one-sentence description, badges for PyPI version, Python version, license, CI status.

Comparison table: the full feature comparison table from the positioning section. This is the first thing developers see after the name.

Install: pip install agentstate-lib. The import line. Optional extras.

Quickstart: a complete 20-line working example using the decorator pattern. Three agents. One edge. Parallel start variant. This example must actually run — test it.

Dashboard screenshot: ![Terminal Dashboard](docs/assets/terminal-dashboard.png) and ![Web Dashboard](docs/assets/web-dashboard.png). Both screenshots must exist before this commit.

Benchmark results: a two-row table showing Llama3-8B success rate without grammar constraints vs with. The actual numbers from your week 6 benchmark run.

Links: Documentation (GitHub Pages URL), PyPI, GitHub Issues.

docs.title: docs/guides/integrations.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — LangChain. Show the make_langchain_agent wrapper. Explain: wrap any LangChain chain in one function. agentstatelib handles coordination; LangChain handles the chain logic.

Section 2 — LangGraph. Show the coexistence pattern. When to use it: you have existing LangGraph code and want to add agentstatelib's event log and conflict detection without rewriting everything.

Section 3 — LiteLLM. Show the LiteLLMAgent subclass. Explain the single-interface value: switch providers by changing one string.

Closing paragraph: "agentstatelib works with everything because it only sees StatePatch objects. It never calls a model, never imports a framework, never touches your API keys. The coordination layer is intentionally separated from the model layer."

docs.title: docs/guides/migration/v0.x-to-v1.md
docs.text:
List every breaking change from v0.x to v1.0. Format each as: What changed, Why it changed, Before (v0.x code), After (v1.0 code).

Breaking changes to document: - submit() now returns (winner, conflict_or_none) tuple instead of just winner - resolve_batch() is now the primary API replacing the sequential submit() pattern - list_workflows() added to StateStore protocol — custom StateStore implementations must add this method - Event type field defaults added — type: Literal["x"] = "x" — construction without passing type= now works

docs.title: Phase 7 · Week 13 · Design Document · Annotated Examples · Docs Review
Context
docs.text: Week 13 is writing week. The design document is the architectural narrative — longer than an ADR, shorter than a paper, written for a technical audience who wants to understand the system as a whole before reading the code. The annotated examples are runnable documentation.

docs.title: Files to work in
docs.text:
1. docs/design.md
  2. examples/workflows/research_pipeline.py
  3. examples/workflows/support_ticket.py
  4. docs/api-reference/observability.md

docs.title: docs/design.md
docs.text:
Write 1500–2000 words. Sections in order:

The problem. What breaks in multi-agent systems without a coordination layer. Be specific: use LangGraph as the concrete example — show the exact failure mode where two parallel LangGraph nodes overwrite each other's state. Show what the user sees (wrong final answer) and what they cannot see (which agent caused it and what state it had). This is the motivating problem your library solves.

Architecture overview. The full pipeline in prose: SharedState (the shared world model, never mutated directly) → StatePatch (the mutation proposal, attributed and reasoned) → AgentGraph run loop (parallel rounds, same snapshot, batch resolution) → ConflictDetector (detect path collisions, resolve, log) → apply_patch (immutable, returns new state) → StateStore (append-only event log) → invariant checkers (post-round consistency verification). Then a second pass explaining the observability layer sitting alongside: every step produces events, events go to the store, the web dashboard reads the store, OTel spans wrap every execution unit.

Key design decisions. One paragraph each for the five ADR decisions, written as narrative not bullet points. Explain the reasoning in plain English. Link to the ADR for readers who want the formal record.

Honest comparison. LangGraph (strong graph abstractions, LangChain ecosystem, no conflict detection or audit trail), CrewAI (good for role-based agent orchestration, conversation-history state model limits observability), AutoGen (strong for conversational multi-agent, no shared world model). Your library: weaker graph construction tools than LangGraph, stronger coordination and observability guarantees. Be honest — if you have weaknesses, naming them shows maturity.

Known limitations. The round-based model requires agents to be side-effect-free within a round (calling the same external API twice with the same input is fine; writing to a shared database is not). The replay model assumes PatchApplied events are sufficient to reconstruct state — external side effects (sending emails, API calls that changed external state) are not captured. SQLiteStore is single-process only.

Future directions. Grammar-constrained output for more providers. A hosted observability platform. Real-time collaboration on shared workflows. Evaluation framework for comparing agent strategies.

docs.title: examples/workflows/research_pipeline.py
docs.text:
Write a complete, runnable research workflow. The topology is planner → [researcher_a, researcher_b in parallel] → writer. Under 80 lines of library code. Every group of related lines has a comment explaining the design decision.

Structure of comments: when you call @graph.node, explain why that agent exists and what its context slice means. When you call graph.edge, explain the condition — what state must exist for this edge to fire and why. When you call graph.run, explain the parallel start semantics if used.

Use stub agents (no real model calls) with realistic return values so the example runs immediately without API keys. The goal field and task descriptions should be realistic — a research question about a real topic.

After the main run: print the event log showing what happened. Print the final state showing all artifacts. Print a WorkflowSummary from analyze_workflow. This makes the example a self-contained demonstration.

docs.title: examples/workflows/support_ticket.py
docs.text:
Different state shape demonstrating the library's generality. A support ticket workflow: priority_classifier → [technical_responder, billing_responder run conditionally in parallel] → response_drafter → quality_checker. Edge conditions route to different agents based on the classification.

Same structure as research_pipeline.py — stub agents, full comments, print event log and summary at the end. The SharedState here uses facts for the ticket message and classification, tasks for the response steps, and artifacts for the drafted response.

docs.title: docs/api-reference/observability.md
docs.text: Autodoc pages for the observability module. Cover: setup_tracing, get_tracer, WorkflowDashboard, WorkflowSummary, analyze_workflow, AnomalyFlag, AgentStats.

docs.title: Docs review pass
docs.text:
Read every page in docs/ as if you are a developer who just discovered the library. For each page ask: can I follow this from top to bottom without getting confused? Does the code example actually run? Are the links correct? Is the information current?

Specific things to check: - Every code example in every doc page must run without errors — test them - Every link in the nav must resolve to an existing file - The quickstart must still work with the current version - The comparison table in index.md must still accurately describe all four libraries

# Live preview while editing
mkdocs serve

# Check all links
mkdocs build --strict

python examples/workflows/research_pipeline.py
python examples/workflows/support_ticket.py

pytest tests/ -v
mypy agentstatelib/ --strict
mkdocs build --strict

# Live docs preview for editing

docs.title: Done when

docs.title: Phase 7 · Week 14 · Blog Posts · Show HN · v1.0.0
Context
docs.text: The last week is communication and final release. The blog posts are engineering content — technical, specific, with real numbers. The Show HN post gets you the feedback you need before calling it 1.0.0.

docs.title: Files to write
docs.text:
1. blog/post-1-why-i-built-agentstatelib.md (publish to dev.to)
  2. blog/post-2-scaling-to-100k-workflows.md (publish to dev.to)

docs.title: Blog post 1: "What happens between LLM calls — why I built agentstatelib"
docs.text:
Opening paragraph. Lead with the benchmark numbers from week 6. Something specific: "When I benchmarked Llama3-8B on producing structured JSON for multi-agent coordination, it succeeded 62% of the time without grammar constraints and 95% with. In a 10-agent workflow, a 62% success rate means on average 4 agents fail and retry on every run." This is a real number from your real experiment. It earns credibility.

Section 1 — The problem I hit. Walk through the specific failure mode you were trying to solve. Two LangGraph parallel nodes, same state key, silent overwrite, wrong answer, no way to debug it. Show the LangGraph code, show the failure, show why there is no way to detect it with LangGraph's current model.

Section 2 — The round model. Explain parallel round-based execution in three paragraphs with a diagram (ASCII is fine). Show the 15-line decorator example. This is the thing developers will copy when they read the post.

Section 3 — Grammar-constrained output. Explain what it is and why it matters. Show the Ollama format parameter. Show the retry-with-correction loop. Give the benchmark numbers. This is the technical section that gets bookmarked.

Section 4 — The observability angle. Show a screenshot of the terminal dashboard. Mention the web dashboard. Explain the replay debugger in one paragraph. This is the section that gets shared by engineering managers.

Closing. Link to the library. Mention it is MIT licensed. Invite feedback on the Show HN thread.

Target length: 1200–1500 words.

docs.title: Blog post 2: "How I would scale agentstatelib to 100,000 workflows per day"
docs.text:
No code. Pure architectural thinking. This post demonstrates that you think like a systems engineer, not just a library author.

Section 1 — The current architecture and its limits. SQLite is single-writer. The FastAPI server is single-process. The event log grows linearly. At what point does each bottleneck appear? Be specific: SQLite handles roughly 1,000–5,000 writes/second on fast SSD. At 10 events per workflow that is 100–500 workflows/second, or roughly 10–40M workflows/day — well above 100k. The actual bottleneck is the model call latency, not the database.

Section 2 — What to instrument first. Before scaling anything, you need to know what is actually slow. Show which metrics matter: p95 latency per agent call, conflict rate per workflow type, retry rate per model, events per workflow. Explain where these come from — your OTel spans and WorkflowSummary already emit most of them.

Section 3 — SQLite to PostgreSQL. Show the one-line change: swap SQLiteStore for PostgreSQLStore. Explain connection pooling with asyncpg. Explain read replicas for the list and get endpoints. Explain why this is the first scaling step, not the last.

Section 4 — In-process to task queue. When the server needs to handle concurrent workflow runs without blocking the HTTP thread, you need a task queue. Show the pattern: HTTP request enqueues a job, a worker pool processes it, SSE stream lets the client watch progress. This separates the HTTP tier from the compute tier.

Section 5 — Single server to worker pool. Multiple FastAPI instances behind a load balancer. The shared state is now the PostgreSQL database — all workers read from and write to the same store. The only coordination issue: two workers processing the same workflow simultaneously. Solve with row-level locking or a distributed lock via Redis.

Section 6 — What you do not change. The library interface. The AgentFn type. The StatePatch model. The event log schema. The round-based execution model. The public API does not change when you scale — only the infrastructure behind it.

Target length: 1000–1200 words.

docs.title: Show HN post
docs.text:
Format: "Show HN: agentstatelib — typed shared state and conflict detection for parallel multi-agent systems (github.com/yourusername/agentstate-lib)"

Body (three paragraphs): what it is and the specific problem it solves (one paragraph). The technical differentiator — conflict detection, event log, replay debugger, web dashboard (one paragraph). The benchmark result and where to find the docs (one paragraph).

Post on a weekday between 9am and 12pm Eastern time — this is when Show HN posts get the most visibility.

docs.title: Version → Docs Mapping
docs.text:
Check this before every publish. Every version must ship with its docs column complete.

Version Feature Docs shipped v0.1.0 SharedState, StatePatch, AgentGraph, SQLiteStore quickstart, concepts/shared-state, concepts/patches, api-reference/state, api-reference/patch, api-reference/graph v0.2.0 Conflicts, parallel execution, invariants concepts/conflict, concepts/parallelism, api-reference/conflicts v0.2.1 FastAPI HTTP layer, list endpoint guides/http-api v0.3.0 Checkpoint, ReplayDebugger, PostgreSQL stub concepts/event-log, guides/checkpoint-recovery, guides/replay-debugger, api-reference/store v0.3.1 LLM integration, local models, benchmark guides/llm-integration, guides/local-models, benchmark.md v0.4.0 OTel, WorkflowSummary, terminal dashboard, web dashboard concepts/observability, guides/opentelemetry, guides/dashboard v0.5.0 Redis cache, rate limiting, Docker, property tests guides/production, guides/testing v1.0.0-alpha Type stubs, ADRs, CI/CD, integrations decisions/, guides/integrations, guides/migration/v0.x-to-v1.md v1.0.0 Final stable release design.md, api-reference/observability.md, full docs review complete

Install: pip install agentstate-lib Import: from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Get your first workflow running in under five minutes.

docs.title: Install
docs.text: pip install agentstate-lib

docs.title: Working Example
docs.text:
import asyncio

from agentstatelib import AgentGraph, SharedState, StatePatch

graph = AgentGraph()


@graph.node("planner", context=["goal"])
async def planner(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="planner",
        target="facts.plan",
        value="research topic",
        reason="Created initial plan",
    )


@graph.node("researcher", context=["facts.plan"])
async def researcher(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="researcher",
        target="facts.research",
        value="Found three useful sources",
        reason="Completed research step",
    )


@graph.node("writer", context=["facts.research"])
async def writer(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="writer",
        target="facts.report",
        value="Final report drafted",
        reason="Converted research into report",
    )


graph.edge(
    "planner",
    "researcher",
    lambda s: s.get("facts", {}).get("plan") == "research topic",
)

graph.edge(
    "researcher",
    "writer",
    lambda s: s.get("facts", {}).get("research")
    == "Found three useful sources",
)


async def main():
    state = SharedState(
        goal="Write a market research report"
    )

    final = await graph.run(
        state,
        start="planner",
    )

    print(final.facts)


asyncio.run(main())

Output:

{
    "plan": "research topic",
    "research": "Found three useful sources",
    "report": "Final report drafted",
}

docs.title: Parallel Agents
docs.text:
Multiple agents can run in the same round.

Pass a list to start=:

final = await graph.run(
    state,
    start=["analyst_a", "analyst_b"],
)

Example:

import asyncio

from agentstatelib import AgentGraph, SharedState, StatePatch

graph = AgentGraph()


@graph.node("analyst_a")
async def analyst_a(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="analyst_a",
        target="facts.a",
        value="complete",
        reason="Analysis A finished",
    )


@graph.node("analyst_b")
async def analyst_b(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="analyst_b",
        target="facts.b",
        value="complete",
        reason="Analysis B finished",
    )


async def main():
    state = SharedState(goal="Run analysis")

    final = await graph.run(
        state,
        start=["analyst_a", "analyst_b"],
    )

    print(final.facts)


asyncio.run(main())

Both agents execute concurrently in round 1 and their patches are batch-resolved before being applied.

docs.title: What's Happening?
docs.text:
• SharedState is the shared world model that all agents read from.
  • @graph.node(...) registers an async function as an agent in the graph.
  • Agents never mutate state directly. They return a StatePatch.
  • A StatePatch is a proposal describing a state change.
  • Edge conditions decide whether downstream agents should run.
  • All agents in a round see the same state snapshot.
  • start=["analyst_a", "analyst_b"] runs both agents in parallel during the first round.
  • Conflicting patches are resolved before any state updates occur.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Learn the core concepts:

  • SharedState
  • Patches

Explore the API:

  • State Models
  • Patch API
  • Graph API

docs.title: How to use this document
docs.text:
Every week has the same structure: - Files to work in — exactly which files, in order - What to write — specific instructions, not code - Docs to write — what user-facing documentation ships with each version - How to test — exact commands to run in PowerShell - How to know it worked — what passing looks like

Three rules: 1. Run test commands at the end of every session, not just end of week 2. Docs ship with every version — users should be able to use what you publish immediately 3. Colab notebook stays running whenever you need local models

docs.title: Documentation Strategy — Read Before Week 0
docs.text: You are publishing to PyPI every few weeks. Users install your library the day you publish. If there are no docs, they cannot use it. Documentation is not a phase 6 activity — it ships incrementally with every version.

docs.title: Doc stack you will use
docs.text:
MkDocs + Material theme — generates a static doc site from Markdown files. Hosted free on GitHub Pages. Updates automatically when you push. This is what FastAPI, Pydantic, and httpx use.

mkdocstrings — reads your Python docstrings and generates API reference pages automatically. Every docstring you write in your source files becomes a searchable API page. This is why docstrings matter from week 1.

mike — manages versioned docs. When you publish v0.1, v0.2, v0.3, users can read docs for the exact version they installed. The landing page shows the latest stable version.

docs.title: Doc site structure (built incrementally)
docs.text:
docs/
├── index.md              ← landing page, competitive comparison table
├── quickstart.md         ← 15-line working example, updated every version
├── concepts/
│   ├── shared-state.md   ← added week 1
│   ├── patches.md        ← added week 2
│   ├── conflict.md       ← added week 3
│   ├── parallelism.md    ← added week 3
│   ├── observability.md  ← added week 7
│   └── local-models.md   ← added week 6
├── api-reference/        ← auto-generated from docstrings by mkdocstrings
│   ├── state.md
│   ├── patch.md
│   ├── graph.md
│   ├── conflicts.md
│   └── ...
├── guides/
│   ├── colab-local-models.md  ← added week 6
│   ├── observability.md       ← added week 7
│   └── migration/             ← added each major version
├── changelog.md          ← CHANGELOG.md symlinked
└── decisions/            ← ADRs, added week 11

docs.title: Where docs live in your repo
docs.text:
agentstateliblib/                ← library source
docs/                      ← MkDocs source files
mkdocs.yml                 ← MkDocs configuration

docs.title: Part A — Library scaffold (same as before)
docs.text:
pyproject.toml — replace entirely. Add [build-system] with hatchling. Add [project]: name agentstatelib-lib, version 0.1.0, description "Typed stateful coordination layer for parallel multi-agent AI systems", requires-python = ">=3.11", dependencies = ["pydantic>=2.0", "aiosqlite>=0.19"]. Add [project.optional-dependencies]: api = ["fastapi>=0.100", "uvicorn[standard]>=0.20"], otel = ["opentelemetry-api", "opentelemetry-sdk", "opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-grpc"], dashboard = ["rich>=13.0"], contrib = [], docs = ["mkdocs-material>=9.0", "mkdocstrings[python]>=0.24", "mike>=2.0"], dev = ["pytest>=7.0", "pytest-asyncio>=0.21", "mypy>=1.0", "ruff>=0.1", "hypothesis>=6.0", "httpx>=0.24"]. Add [tool.ruff], [tool.mypy], [tool.pytest.ini_options] as before.

All __init__.py files — create empty files for agentstatelib/, agentstatelib/core/, agentstatelib/router/, agentstatelib/coordination/, agentstatelib/memory/, agentstatelib/observability/, agentstatelib/api/, agentstatelib/contrib/, tests/, tests/unit/, tests/integration/, tests/property/.

.gitignore — add .venv/, __pycache__/, *.pyc, .mypy_cache/, .ruff_cache/, .pytest_cache/, dist/, *.egg-info/, .env, *.db, .checkpoints/, site/ (MkDocs build output).

.vscode/settings.json — python.defaultInterpreterPath: .venv/Scripts/python.exe, editor.formatOnSave: true, editor.defaultFormatter: charliermarsh.ruff, python.testing.pytestEnabled: true, python.testing.pytestArgs: ["tests"].

docs.title: Part B — Documentation scaffold
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml — create in project root. Set site_name: "agentstatelib", site_description: "Typed stateful coordination layer for parallel multi-agent AI systems", repo_url: https://github.com/yourusername/agentstate, repo_name: agentstate. Set theme: name: material with features: navigation.tabs, navigation.sections, content.code.copy, search.suggest. Set plugins: [search, mkdocstrings: handlers: python: options: show_source: true]. Set nav structure matching the doc tree above (start minimal — just index.md and quickstart.md). Add extra: version: provider: mike.

docs/index.md — write the landing page. Two sentences describing what the library does. Then the competitive comparison table:

| Feature                    | agentstatelib | LangGraph | CrewAI | AutoGen |
|----------------------------|-----------|-----------|--------|----------|
| Typed shared state         | ✅        | ⚠️ dict   | ❌     | ❌     |
| Append-only event log      | ✅        | ❌        | ❌     | ❌     |
| Conflict detection         | ✅        | ❌        | ❌     | ❌     |
| Parallel round execution   | ✅        | ⚠️ limited| ❌     | ❌     |
| Replay debugger            | ✅        | ❌        | ❌     | ❌     |
| Framework agnostic         | ✅        | ❌        | ⚠️     | ⚠️     |
| Local model support        | ✅        | ⚠️        | ⚠️     | ⚠️     |
| Built-in observability     | ✅        | ❌        | ❌     | ❌     |

Then install instructions and a one-paragraph description of the positioning.

docs/quickstart.md — placeholder for now: "Quickstart guide — coming with v0.1.0". You will fill this in at the end of week 2 when you publish.

docs/concepts/ — create empty placeholder files for all concept pages. Each just says "Coming in version X.Y".

docs.title: Part C — Google Colab setup notebook
docs.text:
notebooks/colab_setup.ipynb — create this Jupyter notebook with the following cells. You will open this in Colab Pro whenever you need local model inference.

Cell 1 — Install Ollama and dependencies (run once per session):

!curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
!pip install pyngrok agentstateliblib -q

Cell 2 — Start Ollama server:

import subprocess, time, requests

proc = subprocess.Popen(
    ["ollama", "serve"],
    stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
    stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL
)
time.sleep(3)

try:
    r = requests.get("http://localhost:11434/api/tags", timeout=5)
    print("Ollama running. Models available:", [m["name"] for m in r.json().get("models", [])])
except Exception as e:
    print("Ollama not responding:", e)

Cell 3 — Pull models (run once, cached for session):

import subprocess

gpu_info = subprocess.run(
    ["nvidia-smi", "--query-gpu=name,memory.total", "--format=csv,noheader"],
    capture_output=True, text=True
).stdout.strip()
print("GPU:", gpu_info)

models = ["llama3:8b", "mistral:7b"]
if "A100" in gpu_info:
    models.append("llama3:70b")
    print("A100 detected — will pull 70B model too")

for model in models:
    print(f"Pulling {model}...")
    result = subprocess.run(["ollama", "pull", model], capture_output=True, text=True)
    status = "ready" if result.returncode == 0 else f"FAILED: {result.stderr[:100]}"
    print(f"  {model}: {status}")

Cell 4 — Create ngrok tunnel and print URL:

from pyngrok import ngrok
import json

ngrok.kill()
tunnel = ngrok.connect(11434, "http")
ollama_url = tunnel.public_url

print("=" * 60)
print(f"OLLAMA URL: {ollama_url}")
print("=" * 60)
print()
print("Add this to your Windows .env file:")
print(f"COLAB_OLLAMA_URL={ollama_url}")
print()
print("This URL changes every Colab session.")

Cell 5 — Smoke test from Colab:

import requests, json

r = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:11434/api/generate",
    json={
        "model": "llama3:8b",
        "prompt": 'Return only this JSON: {"target": "facts.test", "value": "hello", "reason": "smoke test"}',
        "stream": False
    }
)
print("Response:", r.json()["response"])
print("Ollama working correctly.")

Cell 6 — Keep-alive (run last, loops forever):

import time
print("Keep-alive running. Do not stop this cell.")
i = 0
while True:
    time.sleep(60)
    i += 1
    if i % 5 == 0:
        print(f"Session alive: {i} minutes")

.env — create on Windows, add to .gitignore. Add:

OPENAI_API_KEY=
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
GROQ_API_KEY=
COLAB_OLLAMA_URL=http://localhost:11434

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/state.py
docs.text:
Every class needs a complete docstring — these become your API reference pages via mkdocstrings.

Write WorkflowStatus as Literal["running", "complete", "failed", "paused"].

Write Task model. Fields: id (str, uuid factory), goal_id (str | None = None), description (str), status (Literal pending/running/done/failed, default pending), result (Any | None = None), created_at (float, time factory), updated_at (float, time factory). Docstring: "A unit of work within a workflow. May optionally belong to a Goal via goal_id."

Write Goal model. Fields: id (str, uuid factory), description (str), status (Literal pending/active/complete/failed, default pending), created_at (float, time factory). Docstring: "A high-level objective that groups related Tasks."

Write Artifact model. Fields: id (str, uuid factory), produced_by (str), artifact_type (str), content (Any), created_at (float, time factory). Docstring: "An output produced by an agent. artifact_type is free-form: 'draft', 'source', 'summary', 'decision', etc."

Write Decision model. Fields: id (str, uuid factory), made_by (str), description (str), rationale (str), timestamp (float, time factory). Docstring: "A recorded decision made during a workflow, with rationale for audit purposes."

Write SharedState model. Fields: workflow_id (str, uuid factory), workflow_type (str, default "general"), goal (str), goals (dict[str, Goal], default empty dict), tasks (dict[str, Task], default empty dict), artifacts (dict[str, Artifact], default empty dict), decisions (list[Decision], default empty list), facts (dict[str, Any], default empty dict), status (WorkflowStatus, default "running"), created_at (float, time factory), updated_at (float, time factory). Docstring must explain: "The single source of truth for a workflow. Never mutated directly — all changes go through StatePatch and apply_patch. All agents in a workflow round read the same SharedState snapshot."

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/events.py
docs.text:
Write BaseStateEvent model. Fields: event_id (str, uuid factory), workflow_id (str), agent_id (str), timestamp (float, time factory). Docstring: "Base class for all events in the agentstatelib event log. Every state change produces an event."

Write six event subclasses. Each needs a type: Literal["event_name"] = "event_name" field plus their specific fields. Docstrings explain what triggers each event.

WorkflowStarted — triggered when AgentGraph.run() begins. Fields: workflow_type, goal.

WorkflowCompleted — triggered when all rounds complete. Fields: final_status.

PatchApplied — triggered after a winning patch is applied. Fields: patch_id, target, old_value: Any, new_value: Any, reason, timestamp. Docstring: "Records the before and after value of a specific state path. old_value and new_value are the values at patch.target only, not full state dumps."

ConflictDetected — triggered when two agents in the same round target the same path. Fields: conflict_id, path, winner_agent_id, loser_agent_id, resolution_strategy, existing_patch: dict[str, object] | None = None, incoming_patch: dict[str, object] | None = None. Docstring: "Emitted when resolve_batch() detects a path collision. The winning patch is applied. Both patches are stored as dicts for serializability."

CheckpointSaved — fields: checkpoint_id, event_count.

AgentErrored — fields: error_type, error_message, retry_count.

Write StateEvent discriminated union using Annotated[Union[...], Field(discriminator="type")]. Write event_adapter = TypeAdapter(StateEvent) at module level.

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/store.py
docs.text:
Write StateStore as a @runtime_checkable Protocol. Four async methods: append, get_workflow, since, count. Each method needs a one-line docstring. Class docstring: "Protocol for agentstatelib persistence backends. Any class implementing these four methods works with AgentGraph. Satisfied by InMemoryStore, SQLiteStore, and user-provided implementations."

Write InMemoryStore. Docstring: "In-memory StateStore implementation. Data is lost when the process exits. Use for testing and development only."

Write SQLiteStore. Docstring: "SQLite-backed StateStore. Events are stored as JSON rows in a local database file. Suitable for single-process workflows. For multi-process or high-throughput workloads, use PostgreSQLStore (install asyncpg)." Every write wrapped in a transaction.

docs.title: docs/concepts/shared-state.md
docs.text:
Write a concept guide explaining SharedState to users. Structure:

Section 1: What SharedState is — the shared world model all agents in a workflow read from. Quote the analogy: a blackboard that all agents can read and propose updates to.

Section 2: Why you never mutate it directly — every change goes through StatePatch → apply_patch → new SharedState object. The original is immutable. Explain what this enables: audit trail, replay, conflict detection.

Section 3: The fields — describe goals, tasks, artifacts, facts, decisions with one example each of when you would use each.

Section 4: Code example — show creating a SharedState, applying a patch, and asserting the original is unchanged. Use literal code blocks that users can copy.

Section 5: What's next — link to the Patches concept page.

docs.title: docs/api-reference/state.md
docs.text:
Write a reference page using mkdocstrings autodoc syntax. Add heading "State Models". Then for each class — SharedState, Task, Goal, Artifact, Decision — add:

## SharedState

::: agentstatelib.core.state.SharedState
    options:
      show_source: false
      members: true

This pulls your docstrings into the rendered page automatically. Users see the class signature, all fields with types, and your docstring.

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/patch.py
docs.text:
Write StatePatch model. Fields: patch_id (str, uuid factory), agent_id (str), target (str), value (Any), reason (str), timestamp (float, time factory), priority (int, default 0). Docstring: "A structured state update proposal from an agent. Agents never write to SharedState directly — they return a StatePatch. The library validates, resolves conflicts, and applies the winning patch. Every field is required except priority."

Write set_nested(obj, path, value) -> dict. Docstring: "Set a value at a dotted path in a nested dict, creating intermediate dicts as needed. Never uses eval()."

Write get_nested(obj, path) -> Any. Docstring: "Get a value at a dotted path in a nested dict. Returns None for any missing intermediate key."

Write apply_patch(state, patch) -> SharedState. Docstring: "Apply a StatePatch to a SharedState, returning a new SharedState. The original state is never mutated. This immutability is what enables event replay and conflict detection."

docs.title: agentstatelib/router/types.py
docs.text:
Write AgentFn = Callable[[dict], Awaitable[StatePatch]]. Module-level docstring: "Type aliases for agentstatelib's agent function interface." AgentFn docstring: "The complete interface for an agentstatelib agent. Any async function accepting a context dict and returning a StatePatch satisfies this type. No inheritance, no base class, no imports from agentstatelib required in agent code."

Write EdgeCondition = Callable[[dict], bool]. Docstring: "A function that receives the current state as a dict and returns True if an edge should fire."

docs.title: agentstatelib/router/context.py
docs.text: Write slice_state(state, include_paths) -> dict. Docstring: "Extract a subset of SharedState by dotted path. Each agent receives only the state paths it declares, not the full state. This limits context window usage for LLM agents and prevents unintended cross-agent information leakage. Empty include_paths returns the full state."

docs.title: agentstatelib/router/graph.py
docs.text:
Use the parallel round-based version from the review session. Every public method needs a complete docstring.

AgentGraph class docstring must explain the execution model clearly: "Round-based parallel agent execution. Each round: (1) collect all agents whose edges fire, (2) run them concurrently against the same state snapshot, (3) batch-resolve any path conflicts, (4) apply winning patches, (5) check invariants, (6) repeat. This guarantees all agents in a round see consistent state and conflicts are resolved explicitly."

run() method docstring: explain Parameters, Returns, Raises. Note that start accepts both str and list[str] for parallel start.

node() decorator docstring: include a full working example in the docstring that users can copy.

edge() docstring: explain that multiple edges from the same source agent cause all reachable agents to run in the next round.

docs.title: docs/concepts/patches.md
docs.text: Write the Patches concept guide. Sections: What a patch is (a proposal, not a write), why agents propose instead of write (audit, conflict, replay), the patch pipeline (agent returns patch → conflict detector → apply_patch → invariants → state), what target dotted paths look like with examples, how priority works in conflict resolution. End with a comparison: "Without agentstatelib, agent B silently overwrites agent A's work. With agentstatelib, the conflict is detected, resolved by your configured strategy, and logged."

docs.title: docs/quickstart.md — fill in completely

This is the most important doc page. Users land here first. Structure:

Install section with pip command.

Working example — a complete 25-line script using the decorator pattern with three sequential agents. The script must actually run. Test it before writing it into the docs.

Parallel agents section — show the same three agents but with analyst_a and analyst_b running in parallel using start=["analyst_a", "analyst_b"].

What's happening section — bullet list explaining each part of the example: what SharedState is, what the decorator does, what StatePatch means, what the edge condition does.

Next steps — links to concept guides.

# Verify the complete example from quickstart.md actually runs
@'
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch

graph = AgentGraph()

@graph.node('planner', context=['goal'])
async def planner(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id='planner',
        target='facts.plan',
        value='done',
        reason='planned',
    )

@graph.node('researcher', context=['facts'])
async def researcher(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id='researcher',
        target='artifacts.result',
        value={
            'produced_by': 'researcher',
            'artifact_type': 'summary',
            'content': 'found',
        },
        reason='researched',
    )

graph.edge('planner', 'researcher', lambda s: s.get('facts', {}).get('plan') == 'done')

async def main():
    state = SharedState(goal='test workflow')
    final = await graph.run(state, start='planner')
    assert final.facts.get('plan') == 'done'
    assert final.artifacts['result'].content == 'found'
    print('quickstart example passes')

asyncio.run(main())
'@ | python

mkdocs build --strict

# Build package
python -m build

submit() now returns (winner, conflict_or_none). Primary API is resolve_batch(patches) -> BatchResolutionResult. All tests use the correct API.

Write eight tests:

test_resolve_batch_no_conflict — different paths, assert result.winners has length 2, result.conflicts is empty.

docs.title: docs/concepts/conflict.md
docs.text:
Write the Conflict Detection concept guide. Sections:

What a conflict is — two agents in the same parallel round both targeting the same state path. Contrast with LangGraph: "In LangGraph, if two agents update the same key, the last writer wins silently. In agentstatelib, the conflict is detected before either patch is applied, resolved explicitly, and logged as a ConflictDetected event."

The resolution pipeline — batch submitted to resolve_batch(), winners returned, ConflictDetected event emitted for each collision, winners applied to state.

Resolution strategies — explain LastWriteWins, PriorityBased, RejectIncoming with when to use each. Show how to set priority on a StatePatch.

Writing a custom resolver — show the ConflictResolver Protocol and a five-line custom implementation.

Reading the conflict log — show how to filter ConflictDetected events from the event log.

docs.title: docs/concepts/parallelism.md
docs.text:
Write the Parallel Execution concept guide. Sections:

The round model — every round all runnable agents execute concurrently against the same state snapshot. No agent in a round sees another's updates until the next round.

Why this matters — eliminates read-write races within a round. Contrast with sequential execution where agent B always sees agent A's changes.

Fan-out topology — how coordinator→[analyst_a, analyst_b] works. Both analysts run in round 2 simultaneously.

Fan-in — how [analyst_a, analyst_b]→aggregator works. Aggregator runs in round 3 after both analysts complete.

Parallel start — run(state, start=["a", "b"]) for when multiple agents should start simultaneously.

The semaphore — max_concurrent parameter in AgentGraph.__init__. Controls total concurrency. Default 10.

docs.title: Test commands for Week 3
docs.text:
# Run all tests — should have 0 failures
pytest tests/ -v

mypy agentstatelib/ --strict

# Verify the parallel model works end to end
python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.core.events import ConflictDetected
from agentstatelib.memory.store import InMemoryStore

async def main():
    store = InMemoryStore()
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)

    @graph.node('analyst_a')
    async def analyst_a(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_a', target='facts.verdict', value='positive', reason='a says positive')

    @graph.node('analyst_b')
    async def analyst_b(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_b', target='facts.verdict', value='negative', reason='b says negative')

    state = SharedState(goal='conflict test')
    final = await graph.run(state, start=['analyst_a', 'analyst_b'])

    events = await store.get_workflow(state.workflow_id)
    conflicts = [e for e in events if isinstance(e, ConflictDetected)]
    print(f'Conflicts: {len(conflicts)}')
    print(f'Winner: {conflicts[0].winner_agent_id}')
    print(f'Final verdict: {final.facts['verdict']}')
    assert len(conflicts) == 1
    print('conflict test passed')

asyncio.run(main())
"

docs.title: agentstatelib/contrib/base_agent.py
docs.text:
Write MaxRetriesExceeded exception carrying agent_id, attempts, last_error, last_output.

Write LLMAgent base class with complete docstring explaining: "Optional base class for LLM-backed agent functions. Provides the retry-with-correction loop used by OllamaAgent, OpenAIAgent, and similar adapters. Not required — any async function returning StatePatch works as an AgentFn."

__init__: agent_id, system_prompt, model, max_retries=3.

__call__: the retry loop. Strip markdown fences. Parse JSON. Validate as StatePatch. On failure: build correction prompt with error message. After max retries: raise MaxRetriesExceeded.

_build_prompt(context, error=None): combine system prompt, JSON context, output instruction. If error: append correction instruction.

_call_model(prompt): abstract, raises NotImplementedError.

docs.title: examples/models/colab_ollama_agent.py
docs.text:
This is the file users copy when they want to run local models from Colab. It must include detailed comments explaining the Colab setup.

Import httpx, json, os. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib. Import LLMAgent from contrib.

Write check_colab_connection(base_url: str) -> bool — GET /api/tags, return True/False with clear error messages.

Write ColabOllamaAgent extending LLMAgent. __init__ reads COLAB_OLLAMA_URL from environment (fallback to localhost). Stores use_grammar: bool = True.

_call_model: POST to {base_url}/api/generate. If use_grammar: include "format": StatePatch.model_json_schema(). Timeout 120 seconds (local models are slow). Return response.json()["response"].

In main(): print setup instructions for Colab, check connection, run a sample call, print result.

Include this comment block at the top of the file explaining the full Colab setup flow so any user who reads it knows exactly what to do:

# COLAB SETUP — how to use this file
#
# 1. Open notebooks/colab_setup.ipynb in Google Colab Pro
# 2. Run all six cells in order
# 3. Copy the ngrok URL printed by Cell 4
# 4. Add to your .env file: COLAB_OLLAMA_URL=https://xxxx.ngrok-free.app
# 5. Run this file: python examples/models/colab_ollama_agent.py
#
# The ngrok URL changes every Colab session. Update .env each time.
# Keep Cell 6 (keep-alive) running while using Colab.

docs.title: examples/benchmark/run_benchmark.py
docs.text:
Write BenchmarkResult dataclass: model, use_grammar, attempt, success, retries_used, latency_seconds, error.

Write async def run_single_attempt(model, base_url, context, use_grammar, max_retries=3). Times the full call including retries. Returns BenchmarkResult.

Write async def benchmark_model(model, base_url, n_runs=20). Runs n_runs with grammar on, n_runs with grammar off. Returns stats dict.

Standard benchmark context:

BENCHMARK_CONTEXT = {
    "goal": "Analyze architectural differences between monolithic and microservices systems",
    "tasks": {"task_1": {"description": "identify three core differences", "status": "pending"}},
    "facts": {"domain": "software architecture", "depth": "technical"}
}

main(): read COLAB_OLLAMA_URL from env. Get model list from BENCHMARK_MODELS env var (default "llama3:8b,mistral:7b"). For each model: run benchmark, print formatted table, save to examples/benchmark/results/{timestamp}.json.

docs.title: docs/guides/local-models.md
docs.text:
Write the local models guide. This is one of your most important pages — it explains your technical differentiator.

Sections:

The problem — local models (Llama3, Mistral) fail to produce valid structured JSON reliably. In a multi-agent system, one malformed output breaks downstream agents. Show a table: without grammar constraints Llama3-8B succeeds ~62% of the time. With grammar constraints ~95%.

How grammar-constrained output works — explain that Ollama's format parameter accepts a JSON schema and constrains sampling to only produce tokens that match that schema. Show how agentstatelib passes StatePatch.model_json_schema() as the format.

The retry-with-correction loop — even with grammar constraints, models sometimes produce wrong values. The retry loop re-prompts with the error message and bad output, giving the model a chance to self-correct.

Using Colab Pro — explain why: your Windows laptop does not have enough VRAM for 70B models. Colab Pro gives you A100 GPUs. Link to the Colab setup guide.

Supported backends — table: Ollama (recommended for Colab), llama.cpp server, vLLM (for high-throughput production), any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

docs.title: docs/guides/colab-setup.md
docs.text:
Step-by-step guide for setting up Colab Pro for local model inference.

Prerequisites section: Colab Pro account, ngrok free account (for the tunnel), Python dotenv.

Step 1: Open the notebook. Explain where notebooks/colab_setup.ipynb is. Tell users to go to File → Open notebook → GitHub → paste the repo URL.

Step 2: Run cells 1–5. Explain what each cell does in one sentence.

Step 3: Copy the URL. Show exactly what the Cell 4 output looks like and where to find the URL in it.

Step 4: Update .env. Show the exact line to add.

Step 5: Verify connection. Give the exact Windows PowerShell command to test.

Step 6: Run keep-alive. Tell users to keep Cell 6 running while they work.

Troubleshooting section: ngrok URL changed (restart Cell 4), Ollama not responding (restart Cell 2), out of VRAM (use smaller model or Q4 quantization), Colab disconnected (re-run all cells, update .env).

Full docstring on module: "OpenTelemetry instrumentation for agentstatelib. Optional — install with pip install agentstatelib[otel]. When OTel is not configured, a no-op tracer is used automatically. Instrumented code in graph.py runs identically with or without OTel."

docs.title: docs/concepts/observability.md
docs.text:
Write the observability concept guide. This is your product story — write it well.

Opening: "Most multi-agent frameworks are black boxes. You know what went in and what came out, but not what happened in between. agentstatelib is the opposite: every agent call, state change, conflict, and routing decision is recorded."

The three pillars — traces (Jaeger), event log (structured history), analysis (WorkflowSummary + anomaly detection).

What Datadog does for infrastructure — brief explanation of APM, distributed tracing, how Datadog traces a request across microservices. Then: "agentstatelib does the equivalent for AI agent workflows. Instead of HTTP requests across services, we trace agent calls across rounds. Instead of database query latency, we track patch application time and conflict rate."

Why no existing library does this — LangGraph has LangSmith but it is tightly coupled to LangChain. CrewAI and AutoGen have no observability layer. agentstatelib is framework-agnostic observability.

docs.title: docs/guides/opentelemetry.md
docs.text:
Step-by-step guide:

Step 1: Install. pip install agentstatelib[otel]. Start Jaeger (give the exact docker command).

Step 2: Configure. Show setup_tracing() call. Explain endpoint parameter.

Step 3: Run a workflow. Existing graph.run() calls automatically emit spans — no code changes.

Step 4: Open Jaeger. Screenshot description (you add actual screenshot later). Explain: service dropdown, trace list, trace waterfall, span attributes.

Step 5: What to look for — which agent is slow, where conflicts cluster, which rounds have the most parallelism.

Connecting to Datadog/Honeycomb — one-paragraph each with the endpoint URL format.

WorkflowSummary section — show analyze_workflow() usage, explain each field.
